close_reason: Added evaluate_expr_with_bindings to Interpreter and updated Runner::eval to inject handle bindings into scope
description:
Currently `eval handle.field` does not work — handles are not in the interpreter's eval scope (confirmed by test at runner.rs:1697). This was explicitly deferred from ttrpg_dsl-avf.

The goal is to make expressions like `eval hero.HP`, `eval hero.Spellcasting.spell_slots`, and `eval hero.Spellcasting` work in the CLI by injecting handle bindings into the interpreter's evaluation context before calling evaluate_expr.

This likely requires either:
1. Pre-processing the expression to substitute handle.field references with their values before eval, or
2. Extending the interpreter to accept an environment of handle→entity bindings so that handle names resolve during expression evaluation

Approach 2 is cleaner but requires interpreter changes. Approach 1 can be done entirely in the CLI crate.
title: Implement eval handle.field access in REPL

close_reason: Fixed with regression tests enabled
description: In crates/ttrpg_parser/src/lower.rs, the synthetic mechanic parameters include user receiver/params (lines 159-166), then the body calls roll(...) using a bare identifier (line 171). The synthesized action also calls the synthetic mechanic by bare identifier (line 219). If a move binding is named roll or equals the generated mechanic name, checker name resolution treats the local binding as the callee and reports not callable; valid moves then fail only after lowering. This is non-hygienic lowering of generated call sites. Lowering should use guaranteed-internal identifiers or proactively reject conflicting move binding names.
title: Move lowering can make generated calls uncallable via name shadowing

close_reason: Already fully implemented: AST has base field on StructLit, parser handles ..base in leading/trailing positions, checker validates base type and suppresses missing field checks, interpreter evaluates base+overrides+defaults. Tests exist in both checker and interpreter.
description:
Structs are immutable value types. To change one field, the entire struct must be reconstructed. There is no spread/update syntax like:
    let upcast_spell = spell { damage: new_damage, ..spell }

This matters for spell upcasting (change damage dice based on slot level), buff modifications, and any case where a struct is 'almost the same' with one field changed.

Rust-style struct update syntax (`..base`) would be a natural fit.
title: Language: struct update syntax

close_reason: All 13 unwrap/expect sites removed from production code. Zero clippy warnings for unwrap_used/expect_used on --lib --bins. All 1368 tests pass.
description: clippy -W clippy::unwrap_used -W clippy::expect_used on --lib --bins reports panic-prone sites in non-test code (examples: ttrpg_lexer/lexer.rs:164, ttrpg_interp/call.rs:997, ttrpg_interp/eval.rs:941/1403/1417, ttrpg_cli/repl.rs:59, ttrpg_cli/runner.rs:1763, ttrpg_cli/completer.rs:93). Replace with structured error handling or explicit invariant checks with diagnostics, and add tests for malformed input / poisoned lock behavior where applicable.
title: Runtime hardening: remove unwrap/expect panics in production paths

close_reason: Added cross-check in collect_entity() to detect field/group name collisions
description: collect.rs tracks entity fields and optional groups in separate duplicate sets, so an entity can have both a field and an optional group with the same name (e.g. Spellcasting). The checker resolves entity.Spellcasting as an optional group before normal fields, while runtime has/field reads use raw field names — creating ambiguous/incorrect behavior. Fix: make field names and optional-group names disjoint per entity during collection.
title: Field/group name collisions allowed in optional groups

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_checker/src/scope.rs

Why this is a bug: ScopeStack::is_group_narrowed and ScopeStack::resolve_group_alias stop scope-walk when scope.bindings.contains_key(var) is true, but var may be a dotted path key such as trigger.actor produced by extract_path_key. Bindings store only root identifiers such as trigger, so root shadowing is not detected for dotted keys. This can make inner scopes incorrectly inherit outer narrowings and aliases for a different shadowed variable.

Potential impact: The checker can accept optional-group access or alias-based group access in inner scopes when it should reject them, causing incorrect type-checking and unsound behavior around has-based narrowing.

Suggested fix: In both methods, derive the root identifier from var (substring before first dot) and stop when either the full key or that root is bound in the current scope. This preserves correct shadowing semantics for both simple and dotted path keys.
title: Bug: Dotted-path group narrowing ignores root shadowing

Add a find(list, predicate) -> option<T> builtin that returns the first matching element or none. Eliminates verbose len(filtered) > 0 / first(filtered).unwrap() boilerplate.

close_reason: Added missing check_name_visible calls for method-style action calls and emit statements
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_stmt.rs

Why this is a bug: check_emit looks up events in self.env.events but never calls check_name_visible with Namespace::Event. In multi-system mode, this allows emitting events defined in other systems without a required use, unlike other event references such as triggers and suppress clauses which enforce visibility.

Potential impact: Programs can compile while violating declared module/system boundaries, causing inconsistent name-resolution behavior and bypassing import rules.

Suggested fix: In check_emit, call self.check_name_visible(event_name, Namespace::Event, span) before or alongside the event lookup, matching visibility checks in other event-reference paths.
title: Bug: emit bypasses event visibility checks

close_reason: Fixed: floor/ceil now reject NaN, infinity, and out-of-range floats with runtime errors instead of silently producing wrong values.
description: builtin_floor and builtin_ceil convert Float to Int via f.floor() as i64 / f.ceil() as i64. Casting float to int with as can silently saturate or coerce edge cases (NaN, +/-inf, very large magnitudes), producing values that may hide invalid input. Add explicit finite/range checks and return runtime errors for invalid conversions, with tests covering NaN/infinity/out-of-range values.
title: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/builtins.rs: guard floor/ceil against non-finite and out-of-range floats

close_reason: Fixed all 4 alias/shadow resolution bugs
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/eval/assign.rs

Why this is a bug: eval_assign_local detects when a local path reaches an Entity and emits MutateField, but unlike eval_assign_entity it never applies resolved_lvalue_aliases from the checker. For valid lvalues that use a group alias on the entity segment, runtime mutation uses the alias token instead of the real group name.

Potential impact: Assignments through local containers (for example wrapper.entity.alias.field -= 1) can mutate the wrong path or become a silent no-op when the alias field does not exist in state, despite passing type checking.

Suggested fix: In the entity branch inside eval_assign_local, apply the same alias rewrite as eval_assign_entity, adjusting the stored segment index by depth before rewriting the emitted path.
title: Bug: Nested-entity assignment skips lvalue alias rewrite

description: When the REPL is in interactive mode (TTY, no --non-interactive flag) and no queued prompt value is available, the CliHandler should pause execution and ask the user for input via stdin. Implementation: (1) Temporarily disable terminal raw mode (crossterm::terminal::disable_raw_mode) so the user gets normal line editing. (2) Print prompt info with color: prompt name, hint text, expected type, and suggested value if any. (3) Read a line from stdin. (4) Parse the input according to return_type (int, string, bool, entity handle). (5) On empty input: use suggest value if available, else UseDefault. (6) On parse error: print a clear colored error message showing what was expected vs what was typed, and re-prompt (loop until valid input or Ctrl-C). (7) On Ctrl-C/EOF: return Vetoed. (8) Re-enable raw mode before returning control to reedline. Use distinct prompt coloring (e.g., yellow/amber) to visually distinguish from the normal green ttrpg prompt.
title: Interactive prompt input via blocking stdin in REPL

description: When DeductCost fires for a payer entity that has no provisioned budget, emit a diagnostic warning. With cost_payer in place, receiver-swap bugs no longer cause costs to charge the wrong entity (cost_payer always routes to the budgeted entity). However, calling a costed action entirely outside a with_budget scope (no budget provisioned at all) should produce a visible diagnostic. Free actions (cost free or cost-less) are legitimate and should NOT warn. The warning surfaces as a diagnostic effect (not an error), giving the host/CLI visibility without breaking execution. Budget enforcement via RequiresCheck (tdsl-h2im) separately causes the action to fail; this warning provides the "why" to help the author find the bug.
title: Warn when costed action fires on entity without provisioned budget

close_reason: Closed
description: Condition modify clauses must enumerate every target function by name. If a new attack mechanic is added, existing conditions silently miss it. Consider a grouping mechanism (e.g. a `trait` or tag on mechanics) so conditions can target all functions in a group. Example: `modify [attack](attacker: bearer)` could target all mechanics tagged as attack resolution, rather than listing resolve_melee_attack and resolve_missile_attack separately.
design:
## Design: Tags & Signature Selectors for Modify Targets

### Tag Declarations

New top-level declaration:

```
tag attack
tag saving_throw
```

Tags applied inline after function signature (before body):

```
mechanic attack_roll(...) -> AttackResult #attack #ranged { ... }
mechanic spell_attack(...) -> AttackResult #attack #ranged { ... }
mechanic saving_throw(...) -> SaveResult #saving_throw { ... }
```

Multiple tags allowed per function. Checker validates:
- Tags on functions must be declared
- Tags in selectors must be declared
- Warn on declared-but-unused tags

### Selector Syntax

Modify clauses accept a bracketed selector instead of a function name:

```
modify [#attack](attacker: bearer) {
    mode = disadvantage
}

modify [returns AttackResult, has attacker: Character](attacker: bearer) {
    mode = disadvantage
}

modify [#attack, has attacker: Character](attacker: bearer) {
    mode = disadvantage
}
```

Selector predicates (AND-only, no OR):

| Predicate            | Matches                                      |
|----------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| `#tag`               | Function has the given tag                    |
| `returns T`          | Return type is T                              |
| `has param: Type`    | Has parameter named `param` of type `Type`    |
| `has param`          | Has parameter named `param` (any type)        |

### Checker: Universal Validation

Treat selector-targeted modify as "for all matched mechanics":

1. Resolve selector to match set M (only derives/mechanics, same as today)
2. If M is empty → warning
3. For each binding in header (e.g. `attacker: bearer`): require every m in M to have parameter `attacker`
4. Require that parameter type to be identical across all m in M (exact match for v1)
5. For each mutation in body (e.g. `mode = disadvantage`): require every return type in M to have `mode` with compatible type
6. Any violation → compile error with per-mechanic diagnostics

No partial application, no silent skips.

### Existing name-based targeting unchanged

```
modify attack_roll(attacker: bearer) { ... }   // still works
```

### Future: Tag Contracts (not in v1)

```
tag attack requires [has attacker: Character, returns AttackResult]
```

Prevents tagging drift — the checker verifies tagged functions conform to the contract.

### Scope

Only derives and mechanics can be tagged (same restriction as modify targets today). Tags on actions/reactions/hooks/prompts are a checker error.
title: Allow conditions to modify functions by trait or pattern

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_parser/src/expr.rs

Why this is a bug: parse_if_let_expr parses the scrutinee with parse_expr() (not parse_expr_no_struct()), and parse_for_expr parses the range end with parse_expr() before immediately expecting a block. In both places, an identifier followed by {} can be consumed as a struct literal instead of stopping before the enclosing block. This rejects valid forms like if let p = x {} and for i in 0..x {} when the body is empty (or begins with tokens that match struct-literal lookahead).

Potential impact: Valid programs are rejected or misparsed in control-flow constructs, especially for empty bodies and certain body starts.

Suggested fix: Use parse_expr_no_struct() for if let scrutinee and for-range end expressions in parse_for_expr, matching the disambiguation used for if conditions, for sources, and match scrutinees.
title: Bug: if-let/for-range can misparse block start as struct literal

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/call/functions.rs

Why this is a bug: dispatch_table_with_values forwards raw Vec<Value> to dispatch_table_core without validating arity, and dispatch_table_core compares keys with arguments using entry.keys.iter().zip(arg_values.iter()). zip truncates to the shorter length, so calls with too few or too many arguments can still match entries instead of erroring. Example: calling a one argument table with zero arguments makes the key loop skip entirely and the first entry can match.

Potential impact: Public API calls through Interpreter.evaluate_derive when targeting a table can return incorrect table results silently on wrong arity inputs, causing incorrect game logic instead of a runtime error.

Suggested fix: Validate arg_values.len() against table parameter count before matching in dispatch_table_with_values or dispatch_table_core, and return a clear arity error on mismatch. Optionally also guard inside core to prevent future callers from bypassing the check.
title: Bug: Table value-dispatch ignores argument arity

close_reason: Implemented auto-fail/auto-succeed patterns via RollMode enum extension and saving_throw mechanic. Both Pattern A (param override) and Pattern B (result override) demonstrated in spec/expanded examples with 6 integration tests.
description:
The modify system only supports advantage/disadvantage as roll modifiers. In 5e, several conditions cause auto-failure on certain saves:
- Stunned: auto-fail STR and DEX saves
- Paralyzed: auto-fail STR and DEX saves
- Petrified: auto-fail STR and DEX saves

The modify clause can set mode=disadvantage but cannot express 'the result is always failure'. Need either:
- A new RollMode variant: `enum RollMode { normal, advantage, disadvantage, auto_fail, auto_succeed }`
- Or the ability to override the result directly in a post-call modify:
    `modify saving_throw(target: bearer) { result = SaveResult.failure }`
title: Language: auto-succeed / auto-fail modifier for rolls

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/eval/emit.rs

Why this is a bug: eval_emit pushes a temporary scope before evaluating event field defaults, but evaluates each default expression with early-return error propagation and only pops the scope after the loop. If any default expression returns a runtime error, the function exits before pop_scope and leaves leaked bindings on the scope stack.

Potential impact: Leaked scope bindings can shadow outer variables and corrupt subsequent evaluation in the same execution context, including error-handling and lifecycle logic that continues using the same Env.

Suggested fix: Wrap the scope-using section in a guard pattern or closure that guarantees pop_scope on all Result paths, matching cleanup patterns used in other eval code.
title: Bug: eval_emit leaks scope on field-default error

close_reason: Refactored PositionValue from Arc<dyn Any> to u64 opaque handles. All 1740 tests pass.
description:
Value::Position currently wraps PositionValue(pub Arc<dyn Any + Send + Sync>). This works for in-process Rust hosts, but it leaks a Rust-specific representation into the runtime boundary and makes future foreign-host, FFI, serialization, and resumable execution work harder.

We should refactor Position to be an explicitly opaque host handle rather than an arbitrary Rust object pointer.

Goals:
- Replace PositionValue(pub Arc<dyn Any + Send + Sync>) with an opaque handle type suitable for serialization/FFI (likely u64 initially).
- Update Value::Position to carry that handle type rather than Any.
- Narrow the host contract so position-specific operations work on position handles directly instead of generic &Value where practical.
- Preserve the semantic rule that the interpreter cannot inspect Position values and must delegate comparison/distance to the host.

Why:
- Removes pointer-identity behavior from the public runtime model.
- Makes the boundary more honest: positions are host-owned opaque references.
- Improves future support for foreign-language hosts and a resumable step API.
- Reduces coupling between interpreter internals and Rust-only host representations.

Likely scope:
- ttrpg_interp::value Position representation
- StateProvider::position_eq / distance signatures and call sites
- reference_state::GameState grid-position implementation
- Tests that currently construct Position values from Arc<dyn Any>

Open design question:
- Use u64 for simplicity, or choose a more process-stable/string-based opaque handle if out-of-process hosts are expected soon. Default recommendation is u64 unless stronger persistence guarantees are needed.

Definition of done:
- No public runtime Position representation depends on Any or pointer identity.
- Host position operations are expressed in terms of opaque handles.
- Existing equality/distance semantics remain host-delegated and tests are updated accordingly.
title: Refactor Position to use opaque host handles instead of Arc<dyn Any>

close_reason: Design complete: Option A (parameterized Grappling condition) chosen. All required DSL features verified working.
description: Design how to track grappling state across rounds: who has the controlling hold (highest initial die roll), inferior hold effects, hold transitions (e.g. arm grab → waist lock), and the option to maintain holds or attempt new grappling attacks. Consider parameterized conditions like Grappling(hold: GrapplingHold, position: HoldPosition). Also consider a language feature bead for default conditions on creature types (e.g. four-legged creatures auto-get FourLegged condition). This is a design task, not implementation.
design:
## Decision: Option A — Parameterized Grappling Condition

### Approach
Single parameterized condition with conditional modify logic. No language changes needed.

### Condition Signature
```ttrpg
condition Grappling(
    opponent: Character,
    hold: GrapplingHold,
    is_controlling: bool,
    hold_roll: int
) on bearer: entity { ... }
```

### Verified DSL Capabilities
- Entity-typed condition params: tested (Frightened(source: Character) in parameterized_conditions.rs)
- Enum-typed condition params: works (enums are values everywhere)
- if/else in modify clauses: ModifyStmt::If in AST, checker, and interpreter (phase 1 + phase 2)
- Mixed required + default params on conditions: tested
- ActiveCondition.params stores BTreeMap<Name, Value> at runtime, including Value::Entity

### State Tracking Design
- Each grappled combatant gets their own Grappling condition with their hold type and roll
- Controlling hold determined by comparing hold_roll between the two combatants' conditions
- opponent field links the two sides of the grapple

### Round-to-Round Behavior
- **Maintain hold**: maintain_grappling_hold mechanic auto-inflicts same damage/effects as initial hold
- **New attack**: New GrapplingAttack replaces existing Grappling condition params (remove old, apply new)
- **DecisiveThrow**: Breaks all existing holds, re-establishes new one

### Hold-Specific Effects (via conditional modify)
- All: suppress spellcasting, weapon restriction (dagger-or-smaller)
- RearChoke (controlling): suppress speech/alarm, ally can force-feed substances
- ArmGrab (controlling): may attempt disarm (saving throw vs paralysis)
- WaistLock/ArmLock/HeadLock (controlling): can disengage + prone opponent
- Inferior holds (ElbowBash, LegHold, HeadButt, WickedElbowBash, Gouge): damage only, no special effects

### Multi-Opponent
OSRIC: 'A combatant can only maintain a hold on one opponent.' Each attacker gets Grappling(opponent: target). Pile-on means multiple attackers each have their own condition instance targeting the same defender. The defender only holds one opponent back.

### Out of Scope (separate beads)
- Default conditions on creature types (e.g. FourLegged auto-applied to horses)
- Fend-off action integration (mechanic exists, action does not)
title: Design: grappling hold state tracking (controlling/inferior holds)

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/event.rs

Why this is a bug: is_suppressed only collects event payload values when the declared type matches Ty::Entity(_). The checker resolves the DSL entity type to Ty::AnyEntity, and suppress clauses are validated against those params/fields. For events using entity-typed params or computed fields, suppression never evaluates conditions on those entities, so matching suppress clauses are silently ignored.

Potential impact: Reactions that should be blocked by condition suppress clauses can still trigger for events that use generic entity params/fields, causing incorrect rules behavior.

Suggested fix: Treat both Ty::Entity(_) and Ty::AnyEntity as entity-bearing in is_suppressed when collecting event params/fields, and require payload values to be Value::Entity in both cases.
title: Bug: Suppress logic skips AnyEntity event values

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_parser/src/lower.rs

Why this is a bug: User-authored move names are copied directly into a synthesized ActionDecl with synthetic=true, and moves are removed before checker collection. Reserved-name validation in checker only runs for non-synthetic actions, so a move named with a reserved '__' prefix (for example, '__hack') is accepted even though user declarations are supposed to reject reserved internal prefixes.

Potential impact: Users can define externally callable declarations in the reserved internal namespace, undermining the reserved-prefix safety rule and increasing risk of collisions/confusing behavior with internal generated names.

Suggested fix: Validate move names against the reserved '__' prefix during lowering (or preserve user-origin metadata so checker still enforces reserved-name rules on lowered user declarations).
title: Bug: Move names can bypass reserved __ prefix validation

close_reason: CLI set now resolves resource bounds and clamps values; compound operators += and -= supported
description: Compound assignment (`-=`) on resource fields bypasses the declared bounds. Example: `hp: resource(0..=max_hp)` with hp=5, `hp -= 6` produces -1 instead of clamping to 0. Plain assignment may also be affected. Resource bounds should be enforced on all mutation paths (direct assignment, compound assignment, action resolve blocks).
title: Resource bounds not enforced on compound assignment

close_reason: Fixed in commit 313628b (Fix 4 REPL/CLI bugs)
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_cli/src/commands.rs

Why this is a bug: In parse_command(), the load branch stops parsing when a token starts with //, assuming it is always a comment marker. Valid paths can begin with // (for example UNC-style paths like //server/share/file), so these inputs are incorrectly parsed as no path and become Unknown.

Potential impact: Legitimate load commands can fail unexpectedly for users with paths that start with //, preventing files from being loaded.

Suggested fix: In the load branch, only treat // as a comment delimiter when it appears after at least one path token, or use quote/comment-aware parsing that does not classify a leading // path token as a comment.
title: Bug: load command misparses paths beginning with //

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_parser/src/expr.rs

Why this is a bug: is_struct_lit_start() only recognizes IDENT { } and IDENT { IDENT : ... } using fixed token offsets. It does not skip Newline tokens after the opening brace. As a result, expressions like Point {\n  x: 1\n} or Point {\n} are not recognized as struct literals even though parse_struct_lit() explicitly supports newlines via skip_newlines().

Potential impact: Valid multiline struct literals are rejected/misparsed, forcing single-line formatting and causing confusing errors in normal code formatting.

Suggested fix: Make struct-literal lookahead newline-tolerant (skip Newline tokens while probing tokens after LBrace), or parse with a tentative/backtracking strategy instead of fixed offsets.
title: Bug: Multiline struct literals are not detected by lookahead

close_reason: Implemented dice(count, sides) builtin
description:
DiceExpr literals are compile-time only. There is no way to construct a dice expression from runtime values. This means upcast spells need a match per slot level:
    match slot_level { 1 => 1d8, 2 => 2d8, 3 => 3d8, ... }

Need something like:
    dice(count: slot_level, sides: 8)  // -> DiceExpr
or a builtin:
    make_dice(slot_level, 8) -> DiceExpr

Also: there is no zero/empty DiceExpr value, so DiceExpr fields cannot have defaults.
title: Language: dynamic dice construction

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/pipeline.rs

Why this is a bug: collect_modifiers_owned iterates modify clauses from ancestor conditions, but check_modify_bindings always looks up the active (child) condition declaration and binds only that receiver name when evaluating clause bindings. If a parent condition uses a different receiver identifier than the child, binding expressions in inherited parent clauses are evaluated with the wrong scope and can fail or mismatch even though the script is valid.

Potential impact: Valid inherited condition modifiers can be skipped or raise runtime errors when receiver names differ across parent/child conditions, causing incorrect modify behavior in production execution.

Suggested fix: Pass the clause’s declaration receiver name (the current cond_decl.receiver_name from collect_modifiers_owned) into check_modify_bindings and bind that name while evaluating bindings for that clause.
title: Bug: Inherited modify bindings use child receiver name

This makes the CLI mutation log incorrect (and can incorrectly append '(clamped)') for missing-field arithmetic, because clamped detection recomputes from old=None (lines 199-210) instead of the actual compute baseline. This is an observability/audit correctness bug in production logs.

description:
The built-in Duration enum has: end_of_turn, start_of_next_turn, rounds(count), minutes(count), indefinite.

Missing duration types needed for 5e:
- 'until next use': Help action grants advantage on the target's NEXT attack/check, then the condition auto-removes. Many buff spells work this way.
- 'until a save is made': Many spell effects (Hold Person, etc.) let the target repeat a save at end of each turn. The condition persists until they succeed.
- 'until concentration ends': Spells like Bless last as long as the caster maintains concentration.

These could be additional Duration variants or a separate mechanism.
title: Language: additional Duration variants

description:
Position is opaque — only `distance(a, b) -> int` is available. Can't express:
- 'Move target 5 feet away from shover' (Shove action)
- 'Is attacker on the opposite side of target from ally?' (Flanking)
- 'All creatures within 20 feet of a point' (AoE targeting)
- Direction vectors, line-of-sight, adjacency

Would need builtins like:
    direction(from: Position, to: Position) -> Direction
    move_toward(pos: Position, target: Position, distance: int) -> Position
    move_away(pos: Position, source: Position, distance: int) -> Position
    creatures_within(center: Position, radius: int) -> list<Character>
design:
## Design Direction

**Core insight**: Spatial operations are always host-resolved. The DSL expresses spatial *intent*; the host decides how to fulfill it — geometry for VTTs, prompting the GM for table-helper apps, or whatever else.

### Why host-resolved?

The host may be a helper app for people playing at a real table with physical minis. It has no position data — it can't compute direction vectors or search for nearby entities. Only the humans looking at the table know spatial relationships. A VTT host has authoritative grid/hex data and can compute everything. The DSL must work for both.

### Existing pattern

`distance(a, b)` already follows this model — it's host-delegated via `StateProvider`. Spatial operations are more of the same mechanism, not a new concept.

### What the DSL needs to express

The three pain points map to two categories:

**Spatial effects** (mutations — tell the host to change the world):
- Shove: `move(target, away_from: actor, distance: 5)` — host repositions the token however it can

**Spatial queries** (predicates/collections — ask the host about the world):
- Flanking: `query flanking(attacker, ally, target) -> bool` — VTT does geometry, table helper asks the GM
- AoE: `query creatures_within(center, radius) -> list<Entity>` — VTT filters by distance, table helper asks GM to select targets

### Consequences

- Spatial operations are **not pure** — they cannot appear in `derive` blocks (correct: you wouldn't derive a field from 'ask the GM if they're flanking')
- They live in the same category as effects, not builtins
- The `StateProvider` trait surface stays lean — spatial resolution goes through effect dispatch

### Future path

1. Add spatial effects for mutations (move/reposition)
2. Add spatial queries for predicates and collections
3. Ship an optional **geometry helper library** that VTT hosts can plug in to auto-resolve spatial queries against grid/hex data (Chebyshev, hex distance, line-of-sight, etc.) so they don't each reimplement the math
title: Language: position arithmetic and spatial queries

description:
Conditions are binary (present or absent) and take no parameters. In 5e:
- Frightened needs a 'source' entity (can't willingly move closer to source)
- Exhaustion has 6 levels with escalating effects
- Grappled needs a reference to the grappler (grappler's speed is also 0)

Would need: `condition Frightened(source: Character) on bearer: Character { ... }`
or: `condition Exhaustion(level: int) on bearer: Character { ... }`

Without this, parameterized effects require separate conditions per case or external state tracking.

close_reason: All 18 sub-issues complete: AST extensions, parser, resolver, metadata types, span rebasing, parse_multi API, MultiSourceMap, checker infrastructure (ownership, visibility, aliases, Ty::ModuleAlias, option extends validation), CLI integration (multi-file load, glob, diagnostics), integration tests, spec update
description:
# Module System Design

## Context

The TTRPG DSL currently processes single files. The `use` keyword is parsed but inert. This design specifies how multiple files compose via system-name-based imports, alias-based qualification, and additive system merging. The deliverable is a spec document and beads issues — no implementation yet.

## Design Decisions

| Decision | Choice |
|---|---|
| Import target | System name (`use "D&D 5e Core"`) |
| Transitivity | No — explicit only |
| Conflicts | **v0**: global duplicate ban (load-time error); **v1**: error on ambiguous unqualified reference (requires qualified access to disambiguate) |
| Qualification | Alias-based (`use "..." as Alias`) |
| File loading | Explicit multi-file + glob (`load *.ttrpg`) |
| System merging | Additive — same-name blocks merge |
| Use scope | All uses in a file apply to all system blocks in that file |
| Cross-system duplicates | **Banned globally in v0** (load-time error) |
| REPL | Unchanged — flat namespace, no ambiguity possible |

### Key Architectural Constraint: Global Name Uniqueness (v0)

In v0, **no two loaded systems may define the same declaration name** in the same namespace. Enforced at module resolution time (before checking).

**Namespaces checked for uniqueness** (matches every flat index in `Program` and `TypeEnv`):
- Types (enums, structs, entities)
- Functions (derives, mechanics, actions, reactions, hooks, prompts — all share the function namespace in TypeEnv)
- Conditions
- Events
- Options
- Enum variants (via `variant_to_enum` — two enums in different systems with overlapping variant names collide)

Same name in different namespaces is fine (e.g., type `Foo` and function `Foo` coexist).

**Consequences**:
- `Program.build_index()` — **unchanged** (flat HashMap, no collisions by construction)
- `TypeEnv` — **unchanged** (flat namespace works)
- Interpreter — **completely unchanged** (trusts checker, uses flat names)
- Qualified access (`Core.foo()`) — **pure syntactic sugar**; checker validates the qualification and resolves to the flat name
- REPL — **no changes**; all loaded declarations visible, no ambiguity possible

**v1 upgrade path**: To allow cross-system duplicates later, `Program` indices change to `HashMap<String, HashMap<String, Decl>>` (system → name → decl). The checker produces a resolution side table `HashMap<Span, (SystemName, DeclName)>` for the interpreter. The v0 `ModuleMap` already tracks per-system ownership, so the infrastructure carries forward.

---

## Language Surface

### Syntax

```
use_decl = "use" STRING ("as" IDENT)? term
```

`as` is a soft keyword (recognized only after a `use` string).

```ttrpg
use "D&D 5e Core"
use "D&D 5e Core" as Core
```

### Qualified Access

With `use "D&D 5e Core" as Core`, the alias `Core` acts as a namespace. Access chains work via the existing field-access typing pipeline:

**Type position**: `Core.Ability` → parsed as `TypeExpr::Qualified { qualifier: "Core", name: "Ability" }`. Checker resolves to `Ty::Enum("Ability")` after validating `Ability` belongs to the aliased system.

**Expression position** — evaluated via the field-access chain:

1. `Core` → `check_ident("Core")` returns new `Ty::ModuleAlias("D&D 5e Core")`
2. `Core.Ability` → `resolve_field(ModuleAlias, "Ability")` → `Ty::EnumType("Ability")` (not `Ty::Enum` — mirrors `check_ident`'s behavior at `check_expr.rs:125` which returns `EnumType` for enum type names used as namespace access)
3. `Core.Ability.STR` → `resolve_field(Ty::EnumType("Ability"), "STR")` → existing variant resolution at `check_expr.rs:570`
4. `Core.modifier(10)` → `check_call` sees `FieldAccess { object: Ident("Core"), field: "modifier" }`, recognizes `Core` as a module alias, dispatches to function `modifier` after validating it belongs to the aliased system
5. `Core.Prone` → `resolve_field(ModuleAlias, "Prone")` → looks up conditions in the aliased system, returns `Ty::Condition`

**Critical distinction**: `resolve_field` for `ModuleAlias` must return `Ty::EnumType` (namespace marker), NOT `Ty::Enum` (value type). The existing checker rejects field access on `Ty::Enum` at `check_expr.rs:593` with "cannot access field on enum value; use pattern matching". Only `Ty::EnumType` supports `.Variant` access at `check_expr.rs:570`.

Since names are globally unique in v0, the types returned are identical to unqualified access — the interpreter needs no changes.

**New `Ty::ModuleAlias(String)`** variant in `ttrpg_checker/src/ty.rs`. Not a "real" type — intermediate marker consumed by `resolve_field` and `check_call`. Cannot appear in function signatures, struct fields, etc.

**Changes to `resolve_field`** (`check_expr.rs:478`): Add `Ty::ModuleAlias(system_name)` arm that mirrors `check_ident` logic (lines 85-138) scoped to the aliased system:
- If field matches an enum TYPE name → return `Ty::EnumType(name)` (for namespace access, not `Ty::Enum`)
- If field matches a bare enum variant → return `Ty::Enum(enum_name)` (fieldless variant as value)
- If field matches a condition name → return `Ty::Condition`
- If field matches a struct/entity name → error: "type cannot be used as a value"
- Otherwise → error: `no declaration "X" in system "..." (alias "...")`

**Changes to `check_call`** (`check_expr.rs`): In the `FieldAccess` branch, before checking for method calls, check if the LHS is a module alias. If so, dispatch the call to the named function after validating system ownership.

### System Block Merging

Multiple system blocks with the same name (same or different files) merge additively. Duplicate declaration names within a merged system are errors.

### Visibility Rules

A system can see:
1. Its own declarations (all fragments merged)
2. Declarations from directly `use`'d systems (not transitive)
3. Builtins (always)

**Name resolution priority**: local scope → own system → imports → builtins

Local declarations always shadow imports (no error).

### Option `extends` Resolution

`option extends "ParentOption"` references another option by name. With the module system:

- The parent option must be **visible** to the extending system (own or imported) — same rules as any other name reference.
- `extends` targets are resolved during the checker's collect pass using the same visibility rules. The existing warning ("option `extends` is not yet validated") is replaced with actual validation:
  1. Parent name exists in the options namespace → ok if visible
  2. Parent name exists but not visible → `error: option "X" extends "Y" which belongs to system "Z" (not imported)`
  3. Parent name does not exist → `error: option "X" extends unknown option "Y"`
- Qualified extends (`extends "Core.ParentOption"`) is **not supported in v0**. The extends field is a plain string name, resolved via flat lookup + visibility check.
- Circular extends (A extends B extends A) should be detected and reported as an error.
- Cross-system extends IS valid when the parent system is imported — this is a key use case (e.g., a supplement extending a core ruleset's options).

### Use Scoping Within Files

All `use` declarations in a file apply to ALL system blocks in that file. When a system spans multiple files, its imports are the union of uses from all files containing it.

**Design tradeoff**: This means if a file defines systems A and B, and has `use "C"`, then both A and B can see C's declarations — even if the import was only intended for A. This is a deliberate simplification for v0:
- It mirrors how most DSLs and configuration languages scope imports (file-level, not block-level).
- Placing `use` inside a `system` block would require parser changes and creates awkward nesting for the common case where a file has one system.
- The practical workaround is simple: put unrelated systems in separate files.
- If per-system scoping is needed later, `use` inside `system { }` can be added as a refinement without breaking file-level `use`.

### Error Cases

| Scenario | Diagnostic |
|---|---|
| `use "Unknown"` | `error: unknown system "Unknown"` |
| Two systems define `modifier` | `error: duplicate declaration "modifier": defined in "Core" (core.ttrpg:5) and "Alt" (alt.ttrpg:3)` |
| Two systems define enum variant `STR` | `error: duplicate enum variant "STR": defined in "Ability" (system "Core") and "Stat" (system "Alt")` |
| `use "A" as X` + `use "B" as X` | `error: duplicate alias "X": already used for "A"` (same alias → different targets) |
| `use "A" as X` in two files for same system | Idempotent — no error (same alias → same target) |
| Alias collides with own declaration | `error: alias "Foo" conflicts with declaration "Foo"` |
| Alias collides with imported name | `error: alias "modifier" shadows imported declaration "modifier"` |
| Alias collides with builtin | `error: alias "floor" shadows builtin function "floor"` (checked by checker, not resolver — builtins are defined in `ttrpg_checker::builtins`) |
| Self-import | warning (no-op) |
| `use` with no system block in file | warning |
| Duplicate decl within merged system | `error: duplicate declaration "Ability" in system "D&D 5e Core"` |
| `Core.nonexistent` | `error: no declaration "nonexistent" in system "D&D 5e Core" (alias "Core")` |
| Unqualified name not visible | `error: "modifier" is not visible; it belongs to system "Core" which is not imported` |
| `option extends` parent not visible | `error: option "X" extends "Y" which belongs to system "Z" (not imported)` |
| `option extends` unknown parent | `error: option "X" extends unknown option "Y"` |
| `option extends` circular | `error: circular option extends: "A" → "B" → "A"` |
| Circular `use` (A↔B) | Allowed — no ordering dependency |

---

## Architecture Changes

### Pipeline

```
CLI reads files → parse_multi (parse + lower_moves + resolve) → check_with_modules → interpret
```

`parse_multi` encapsulates the entire pre-check pipeline:
1. Parse each file independently
2. **`lower_moves` per file** — before resolution, so synthetic declarations (e.g., `__attack_roll`) are visible to the resolver and included in `ModuleMap` ownership/visibility
3. Compute base offsets and **rebase all spans + diagnostics to global offsets**
4. Merge programs into single `Program`
5. Resolve modules (registry, validation, collision detection, visibility, desugar)
6. Return merged Program + ModuleMap + all diagnostics (all with global offsets)

There is no separate `lower_moves` call in the CLI — it is part of `parse_multi`. The CLI calls `parse_multi` then `check_with_modules`.

**Fail-fast on resolution errors**: If `parse_multi` returns any error-level diagnostics (parse errors, duplicate declarations, unknown imports, etc.), the CLI must **not** proceed to `check_with_modules` or interpretation. The merged `Program` may contain duplicate names where `build_index()` silently overwrote a HashMap entry, making downstream analysis unsound. The CLI stores the diagnostics for `errors` display and reports the error count, but does not set the program/type_env as loaded. This is the same pattern the CLI already uses for parse errors today — it's extended to cover resolution errors.

Module resolution is a **language-level** concern in `ttrpg_parser`, not a CLI concern.

### ttrpg_ast (`crates/ttrpg_ast/src/ast.rs`)

**UseDecl** gains alias + span:
```rust
pub struct UseDecl {
    pub path: String,
    pub alias: Option<String>,
    pub span: Span,
}
```

**TypeExpr** gains qualified variant:
```rust
TypeExpr::Qualified { qualifier: String, name: String }
```

**New module metadata types** (new file `crates/ttrpg_ast/src/module.rs`):
```rust
pub struct ModuleMap {
    pub systems: HashMap<String, SystemInfo>,
}

pub struct SystemInfo {
    // Per-namespace declaration names — mirrors TypeEnv's namespace split
    pub types: HashSet<String>,
    pub functions: HashSet<String>,   // derives, mechanics, actions, reactions, hooks, prompts
    pub conditions: HashSet<String>,
    pub events: HashSet<String>,
    pub options: HashSet<String>,
    pub variants: HashSet<String>,    // enum variant names

    pub imports: Vec<ImportInfo>,
}

pub struct ImportInfo {
    pub system_name: String,
    pub alias: Option<String>,
    pub span: Span,
}
```

**Program and build_index** — **no changes**. Flat index remains runtime truth.

### ttrpg_parser (`crates/ttrpg_parser/src/`)

**`parser.rs`** — update `parse_use_decl` to handle `as IDENT` soft keyword.

**New `resolve.rs` module**:
```rust
/// Takes already-rebased, merged program. Produces ModuleMap + validation diagnostics.
/// All spans in the input program are already global offsets.
pub fn resolve_modules(
    program: &mut Program,           // merged, rebased — mutated to desugar TypeExpr::Qualified
    file_systems: Vec<FileSystemInfo>, // which systems and use-decls came from which files
) -> (ModuleMap, Vec<Diagnostic>)
```

`FileSystemInfo` carries: filename, list of system names defined in the file, list of UseDecls in the file. This is extracted by `parse_multi` before calling `resolve_modules`.

**Algorithm — split between `parse_multi` and `resolve_modules`**:

`parse_multi` (steps 1-3):
1. **Parse + lower each file**: For each source, `parse()` then `lower_moves()`. Collect per-file programs and diagnostics.
2. **Rebase spans**: Compute base offsets (with 1-byte sentinel gaps). Rebase ALL AST spans and ALL diagnostic spans for each file to global offsets.
3. **Merge programs**: Concatenate rebased items into a single `Program`, call `build_index()`. Extract `FileSystemInfo` per file.

`resolve_modules` (steps 4-9, all spans already global):
4. **Build system registry**: `HashMap<String, Vec<(file_idx, Vec<Spanned<DeclKind>>)>>`. Detect duplicate declarations within merged same-name systems.
5. **Collect per-file `use` declarations**: Associate with all system blocks in that file (union).
6. **Validate imports**: Use targets exist, alias collisions, self-imports. Alias collision rule: same alias → same target across files is **idempotent** (no error); same alias → different targets is an error. Also check alias vs own declarations and alias vs imported names. Alias-vs-builtin deferred to checker (builtins live in `ttrpg_checker::builtins`, not accessible from parser).
7. **Detect global name collisions** across all namespaces (types, functions, conditions, events, options, enum variants). Error with both source locations.
8. **Compute per-system visibility** (own + direct imports).
9. **Desugar `TypeExpr::Qualified`**: Walk all AST nodes in `program.items` and replace `TypeExpr::Qualified { qualifier, name }` with `TypeExpr::Named(name)`, after validating the alias and verifying the type belongs to the aliased system. This must happen before `collect()` because `TypeEnv::resolve_type()` (`env.rs:146`) runs during pass 1b signature resolution and has no system context. Safe because names are globally unique in v0.
10. **Return** ModuleMap + validation diagnostics (all with global offsets).

**New `parse_multi` public API** in `lib.rs`:
```rust
pub struct ParseMultiResult {
    pub program: Program,
    pub module_map: ModuleMap,
    pub diagnostics: Vec<Diagnostic>,
    pub has_errors: bool,  // true if any diagnostic is error-level
}

pub fn parse_multi(
    sources: &[(String, String)],  // (filename, source_text)
) -> ParseMultiResult
```

**Safe consumption pattern**: `ParseMultiResult` includes `has_errors` so callers can check before proceeding. The `program` and `module_map` are always returned (even with errors) for diagnostic rendering and IDE use cases.

To prevent accidentally checking a program with duplicate names, `ParseMultiResult` provides a guarded accessor:

```rust
impl ParseMultiResult {
    /// Returns program + module_map only if no errors.
    /// CLI and other callers use this before calling check_with_modules.
    pub fn ok(&self) -> Option<(&Program, &ModuleMap)> {
        if self.has_errors { None } else { Some((&self.program, &self.module_map)) }
    }
}
```

The checker's API remains decoupled from the parser — it takes `&Program` and `&ModuleMap` separately:

```rust
// ttrpg_checker::lib.rs — unchanged layering
pub fn check_with_modules(program: &Program, modules: &ModuleMap) -> CheckResult
```

The fail-fast guard lives in the calling code (CLI), not in the checker. This keeps `ttrpg_checker` independently usable — test code can construct a `Program` + `ModuleMap` directly without going through `parse_multi`.

**Diagnostic span rebasing**: Parse and lower diagnostics from file N have local-offset spans. In `parse_multi`, span rebasing (algorithm step 2) applies to ALL spans — both AST node spans and diagnostic spans — BEFORE any validation or collision detection. This ensures:
- Parse diagnostics: rebased alongside their file's AST spans
- Lower diagnostics: rebased alongside their file's AST spans
- Resolution diagnostics (steps 4-9): produced with global offsets naturally, since they reference already-rebased AST spans

**`types.rs`** — update type parsing to handle `IDENT "." IDENT` as `TypeExpr::Qualified`.

### Multi-File Span Handling

**`Span` type is unchanged** (`{ start: usize, end: usize }`).

**Span rebasing during merge**: Each file gets a base offset with a **1-byte sentinel gap** between files to avoid boundary ambiguity (EOF of file N at offset `base_N + len(source_N)` would otherwise collide with start of file N+1):
- File 0: base = 0
- File 1: base = len(source_0) + 1
- File 2: base = len(source_0) + 1 + len(source_1) + 1
- General: base_N = sum(len(source_i) + 1 for i in 0..N)

The sentinel byte is a logical gap — it does not appear in any source text or span. It ensures that a span at exactly EOF of file N (`base_N + len(source_N)`) cannot be attributed to file N+1 (`base_{N+1}`).

No physical source concatenation occurs.

**New `MultiSourceMap`** in `crates/ttrpg_ast/src/diagnostic.rs`:

```rust
/// Renders diagnostics across multiple source files.
/// Owns source strings to avoid self-referential borrowing in the host.
pub struct MultiSourceMap {
    files: Vec<FileEntry>,
}

struct FileEntry {
    filename: String,
    base_offset: usize,
    /// Half-open upper bound: base_offset + source.len().
    /// Valid span starts satisfy: base_offset <= start <= end_exclusive.
    /// The `<=` on the right allows zero-width EOF spans (start == end_exclusive).
    /// The 1-byte sentinel gap guarantees end_exclusive < next file's base_offset.
    end_exclusive: usize,
    source: String,       // owned — avoids self-borrow issues in Runner
    line_starts: Vec<usize>,
}
```

**Owning design**: `MultiSourceMap` stores owned `String` sources (moved or cloned from the loaded file contents). This avoids the self-referential struct problem where `Runner` would need to store both `Vec<(String, String)>` sources and a `MultiSourceMap<'a>` borrowing from them — that pattern is not representable in safe Rust.

`MultiSourceMap` does NOT wrap `SourceMap` instances. It reimplements line/col computation with its own `line_starts` per file. The rendering logic from `SourceMap::render` is factored into a shared free function or duplicated (it's ~15 lines).

`MultiSourceMap::render(diag)`:
1. Find which file owns the span: iterate `files`, match on `base_offset <= span.start <= end_exclusive`. This is a closed check on both sides: `end_exclusive` equals `base_offset + source.len()`, and a span at exactly that offset is a zero-width EOF span (e.g., "unexpected end of file") that still belongs to this file. The 1-byte sentinel gap guarantees `end_exclusive < next_file.base_offset`, so no ambiguity.
2. Subtract the file's `base_offset` to get local span offsets
3. Compute line/col from the file's `line_starts` and `source`
4. Render with `filename:line:col` prefix (extends the existing rustc-style format)

**Uniform usage**: `MultiSourceMap` is constructed for ALL `load` commands, including single-file loads (it works fine with one file entry). This means all `load`-sourced diagnostics use the rich `filename:line:col` format uniformly. The legacy `[error] message` format (no spans) is only used for REPL `eval` errors and other contexts where no source map exists. Existing `SourceMap<'a>` remains available for non-CLI consumers (tests, library callers).

### ttrpg_checker (`crates/ttrpg_checker/src/`)

**New entry point** in `lib.rs`:
```rust
pub fn check_with_modules(program: &Program, modules: &ModuleMap) -> CheckResult
```

Takes `&Program` and `&ModuleMap` directly — no dependency on `ParseMultiResult`. Callers are responsible for not passing programs with duplicate names (enforced by `ParseMultiResult::ok()` guard in the CLI). The existing `check(program)` remains for backward compatibility (single-file, no module constraints).

**`collect.rs`** — `collect()` accepts optional `&ModuleMap`. During collection, tracks which system owns each declaration:
```rust
// New fields in TypeEnv — one owner map per namespace
pub type_owner: HashMap<String, String>,      // decl name → system name
pub function_owner: HashMap<String, String>,
pub condition_owner: HashMap<String, String>,
pub event_owner: HashMap<String, String>,
pub option_owner: HashMap<String, String>,
pub variant_owner: HashMap<String, String>,   // variant name → system name (via owning enum)

pub system_visibility: HashMap<String, VisibleNames>,
pub system_aliases: HashMap<String, HashMap<String, String>>, // system → {alias → target_system}
```

Every namespace that has a global uniqueness constraint must have an owner map. The `option_owner` map is required for `extends` visibility checks. The `variant_owner` map is required for qualified access to bare variants (`Core.STR`) and for "not visible" diagnostics that name the owning system.

`VisibleNames` per system:
```rust
pub struct VisibleNames {
    pub types: HashSet<String>,
    pub functions: HashSet<String>,
    pub conditions: HashSet<String>,
    pub events: HashSet<String>,
    pub variants: HashSet<String>,
    pub options: HashSet<String>,
}
```

Computed as: own declarations ∪ (union of all directly imported systems' declarations).

**`ty.rs`** — add `Ty::ModuleAlias(String)`:
- Intermediate marker type for module alias identifiers
- Cannot appear in function signatures, struct fields, etc.
- Consumed by `resolve_field` and `check_call`

**`check.rs`** — `Checker` gains `current_system: Option<String>`, set when entering each system block. Name lookups are gated:

```rust
fn is_name_visible(&self, name: &str, namespace: Namespace) -> bool {
    let system = match &self.current_system {
        Some(s) => s,
        None => return true, // REPL / single-file mode: everything visible
    };
    match self.env.system_visibility.get(system) {
        Some(vis) => vis.contains(name, namespace),
        None => true, // no module info: legacy mode
    }
}
```

**Comprehensive visibility gating** — the checker resolves names in several places beyond `check_expr.rs` and `check_pattern.rs`. Each must be gated by `is_name_visible` to prevent module-visibility bypasses:

| Call site | What's looked up | Gate needed |
|---|---|---|
| `check.rs:202` `check_trigger_and_body` | `self.env.events.get(&trigger.event_name)` — trigger event | `is_name_visible(event_name, Namespace::Event)` before lookup |
| `check_modify.rs:22` `check_modify_clause` | `self.env.lookup_fn(&clause.target)` — modify target fn | `is_name_visible(target, Namespace::Function)` before lookup |
| `check_modify.rs:278` `check_suppress_clause` | `self.env.events.get(&clause.event_name)` — suppress event | `is_name_visible(event_name, Namespace::Event)` before lookup |
| `check_stmt.rs:317,413` `check_narrowing` | `self.env.lookup_optional_group(entity, group)` — optional group | Entity type already resolved via `resolve_type` which is visibility-gated (see below); group names are entity-internal, not system-scoped — **no extra gate needed** |

For all gated sites: if the name exists in the env but is not visible, emit `error: "X" is not visible; it belongs to system "Y" which is not imported` (same message as check_expr). If the name doesn't exist at all, fall through to the existing "not defined" error.

**`env.rs` — visibility-aware `resolve_type`**: `resolve_type` is called from ~20 sites across `check.rs`, `check_stmt.rs`, `check_modify.rs`, and `collect.rs` (let annotations, function signatures, receiver types, parameter types, field types, etc.). Rather than gating every callsite individually, `resolve_type` itself becomes visibility-aware:

```rust
// New method on TypeEnv
pub fn resolve_type_visible(
    &self,
    type_expr: &Spanned<TypeExpr>,
    current_system: Option<&str>,
    visibility: Option<&HashMap<String, VisibleNames>>,
) -> (Ty, Option<Diagnostic>)
```

When `current_system` is `Some` and the resolved type name is not visible, returns `(Ty::Error, Some(diagnostic))`. When `current_system` is `None` (REPL/single-file), behaves identically to existing `resolve_type`. The existing `resolve_type(&self, expr) -> Ty` remains as a convenience wrapper that passes `None` for backward compatibility.

The `Checker` calls `resolve_type_visible` with its `current_system`, collecting any emitted diagnostic. This covers ALL type annotation paths uniformly:
- `check_stmt.rs:33` — `let x: Type = ...`
- `check.rs:78,87` — derive/mechanic return types
- `check.rs:96,148,175` — action/reaction/hook receiver types
- `check.rs:355,419,495` — function parameter types
- `check.rs:440,460,477` — struct/entity field types
- `check_modify.rs:49,281` — condition receiver types
- `check_modify.rs:69,157,293` — parameter/annotation types in modify/suppress

**Other `env.rs` methods**:
- `lookup_fn` at `env.rs:231`: Remains visibility-unaware. Gated at each callsite (`check_call` in check_expr.rs, `check_modify_clause` in check_modify.rs) where the caller has `current_system` context.
- `lookup_fields` at `env.rs:237`: Called for struct literal checking — the struct type name was already visibility-checked when the type was resolved, so field access on an already-validated type does not need a second gate.
- `lookup_optional_group` at `env.rs:246`: Entity type already visibility-checked via `resolve_type_visible`.

**`collect.rs`** — **alias-vs-builtin validation**: After `register_builtin_types` and builtin function registration (end of collect pass), if a `ModuleMap` is provided, iterate all alias names from `system_aliases` and check each against the builtin function table (`BUILTIN_FUNCTIONS` or equivalent lookup). Emit `error: alias "floor" shadows builtin function "floor"` for any collision. This is an **eager, declaration-site check** — it fires even if the alias is never used, matching the behavior of alias-vs-own-declaration and alias-vs-import collisions (which are also declaration-site errors in `resolve_modules`). The check lives in the checker because builtins are defined in `ttrpg_checker::builtins`, not accessible from the parser.

**`check_expr.rs`** — four changes:
1. **`check_ident`**: After local scope and before enum/variant/condition checks, if name matches a module alias for `current_system`, return `Ty::ModuleAlias(system_name)`. Aliases are validated at resolution time to not shadow own declarations or imported names, so this insertion point cannot silently shadow a name found later in the lookup chain. Alias-vs-builtin is checked eagerly in `collect.rs` (see above), so no shadowing can occur at lookup time.
2. **`resolve_field`**: Add `Ty::ModuleAlias(system_name)` arm — look up the field in the aliased system's declarations across all namespaces (types, conditions, enum variants, etc.) and return the appropriate type.
3. **`check_call` FieldAccess branch**: Before existing enum-variant-constructor logic, check if LHS identifier is a module alias. If so, validate the field name belongs to the aliased system and dispatch to normal function checking.
4. **`check_call` direct calls**: When resolving a function name (at `check_expr.rs:762+`), visibility must be checked without breaking builtin fallback. Current `lookup_fn` does user-fn-first → builtin-second (`env.rs:231`). The visibility-aware lookup must follow this priority:
   - Visible user fn (own + imported) → use it
   - Builtin → use it
   - Non-visible user fn exists → error: "not visible; belongs to system X which is not imported"
   - Not found → error: "undefined"

   A non-visible user function must NOT shadow a builtin. For example, if system "A" defines `floor()` and system "B" doesn't import "A", calling `floor()` in B should resolve to the builtin, not error with "floor is not visible." The same logic applies to conditions and any other namespace with builtin entries.
5. **`check_pattern`**: Pattern matching resolves enum names/variants from global env (`check_pattern.rs:85`, `check_pattern.rs:142`). Add `is_name_visible` checks when resolving enum/variant names in pattern position to close this visibility gap.

**`collect.rs`** — visibility for type signatures: During pass 1b signature resolution, the collector calls `resolve_type_visible` (see env.rs above) with `current_system` set to the system being collected. The collector already iterates system blocks, so it knows which system it's processing. This gates ALL type references in function signatures, field types, parameter types, etc. Without this, a function signature could reference a type from a non-imported system and the error would only appear if the type happened to be used in the function body.

### ttrpg_interp — **No changes in v0**

Since names are globally unique, qualified access is resolved to unqualified names by the checker, and the flat `Program`/`TypeEnv` indices work unchanged.

### ttrpg_cli (`crates/ttrpg_cli/src/`)

**`commands.rs`** — `load` currently takes only the first token (line 51: `let (path, _) = split_first_token(tail_trimmed)`). This must change to pass all path tokens. **Critical**: `strip_comment` must NOT be used for load paths because `//` can appear in valid file paths (see `commands.rs:36-38` and test at line 307). Instead, tokenize by whitespace and stop collecting tokens when a token starts with `//`:

```rust
"load" => {
    let tail_trimmed = tail.trim_start();
    // Collect all whitespace-delimited tokens until one starts with "//"
    let mut paths = String::new();
    for token in tail_trimmed.split_whitespace() {
        if token.starts_with("//") {
            break; // rest is a comment
        }
        if !paths.is_empty() {
            paths.push(' ');
        }
        paths.push_str(token);
    }
    if paths.is_empty() {
        Some(Command::Unknown("load".into()))
    } else {
        Some(Command::Load(paths))
    }
}
```

This preserves `//` within paths (e.g., `/tmp//file.ttrpg` is a single token) while stripping trailing `// comment` (which starts a new token with `//`).

**Known limitation**: Paths that START with `//` (e.g., UNC paths `//server/share/file.ttrpg`) will be treated as comment markers. This is acceptable for v0 — TTRPG DSL files do not use UNC paths in practice. If needed, a future version could support quoted paths (`load "//path"`).

**`runner.rs`** — `cmd_load` changes:
```rust
fn cmd_load(&mut self, tail: &str) -> Result<(), CliError> {
    // 1. Split tail into whitespace-delimited tokens
    // 2. For each token: if it contains glob chars (* ? [), expand with `glob` crate
    //    and sort the expanded results alphabetically for determinism
    //    Otherwise treat as literal path (preserving user-specified order)
    // 3. Dedup while preserving first occurrence
    //    (user order matters: reactions/options maintain declaration order)
    // 4. Read all files into Vec<(String, String)>
    // 5. Call ttrpg_parser::parse_multi(&sources) → ParseMultiResult
    //    (this handles parse + lower_moves + resolve internally)
    // 6. If parse_result.ok() returns Some((program, module_map)):
    //       call ttrpg_checker::check_with_modules(program, module_map)
    //    Else: store parse diagnostics, skip checking
    // 7. Construct owning MultiSourceMap from sources
    // 8. Store program, type_env, diagnostics, last_paths, multi_source_map
}
```

**Path ordering**: Explicit paths preserve user order (`load a.ttrpg b.ttrpg` → a first, then b). Glob expansions are sorted alphabetically within each glob for determinism, then inserted at the glob's position in the argument list. This matters because `reaction_order`, `option_order`, and `hook_order` depend on declaration order.

**Glob failure modes**:
- Glob matches zero files → **hard error**: `error: no files matched pattern "*.foo"`. This is not silent because a zero-match glob almost certainly indicates a typo or wrong directory, and silently loading nothing would produce confusing downstream errors (e.g., "unknown system" when the user thought the file was loaded).
- Glob syntax error (e.g., unclosed `[`) → hard error from the `glob` crate, propagated as `error: invalid glob pattern "..."`.
- Literal path does not exist → hard error: `error: file not found: "missing.ttrpg"` (existing behavior, unchanged).
- Glob matches files but some are unreadable → hard error on the unreadable file (fail-fast, not partial load).

`last_path: Option<PathBuf>` → `last_paths: Vec<PathBuf>` for `reload`.

**`runner.rs` diagnostic rendering** (line 341-351) — currently `cmd_errors` prints `[severity] message` without spans. After a `load` or `reload`, a `MultiSourceMap` is always constructed (even for single-file loads), so diagnostics use `filename:line:col` with caret underlines uniformly:

```rust
// New field on Runner (owning, no lifetime parameter needed)
multi_source_map: Option<MultiSourceMap>,

// In cmd_errors:
if let Some(ref map) = self.multi_source_map {
    for diag in &self.diagnostics {
        self.output.push(map.render(diag));
    }
} else {
    // Fallback: [severity] message (before any file is loaded)
    for diag in &self.diagnostics {
        self.output.push(format!("[{}] {}", severity_str(diag), diag.message));
    }
}
```

Since `MultiSourceMap` owns its source strings, no self-referential borrowing issues arise. The fallback path is only hit when no file has been loaded (edge case). Note: REPL `eval` errors do **not** flow through `self.diagnostics` — they are returned directly as `CliError::Message` from the `eval()` method (`runner.rs:207-244`). The `cmd_errors` command only shows diagnostics from file loading (parse/check errors), not from interactive eval.

**Dependency**: Add `glob` crate to `ttrpg_cli/Cargo.toml`.

## Implementation Phases (Beads Issues)

### Phase 1: AST + Parser Foundation
1. **Extend UseDecl** — add `alias: Option<String>`, `span: Span` fields in `ast.rs`
2. **Parse `use ... as`** — soft keyword `as` in `parser.rs`, update `parse_use_decl`
3. **Add TypeExpr::Qualified** — new variant, parse `IDENT.IDENT` in type position (`types.rs`)

### Phase 2: Module Metadata + Resolution
4. **Module metadata types** — new `module.rs` in ttrpg_ast: `ModuleMap`, `SystemInfo`, `ImportInfo`
5. **Module resolver** — new `resolve.rs` in ttrpg_parser: system registry, merging, validation, global duplicate detection across all namespaces, visibility computation, **TypeExpr::Qualified desugaring** (rewrite to TypeExpr::Named before collect)
6. **Multi-file parse API** — `parse_multi()` in ttrpg_parser `lib.rs`

### Phase 3: Multi-File Diagnostics
7. **Span rebasing** — offset spans during `parse_multi` (step 2), before program merge
8. **MultiSourceMap** — new owning struct in `diagnostic.rs` (owns `String` sources + `line_starts` per file); does NOT wrap `SourceMap` instances

### Phase 4: Checker Module Awareness
9. **Declaration ownership tracking** — collector records which system owns each name in TypeEnv
10. **`Ty::ModuleAlias`** — new intermediate marker type in `ty.rs`
11. **Visibility enforcement** — checker gates lookups via `is_name_visible()`, reports "not visible" errors. Gates in: `check_expr` (expressions/calls), `check_pattern` (patterns), `check.rs` (trigger events), `check_modify.rs` (modify targets, suppress events), `collect.rs` (type signatures in pass 1b). Also: **alias-vs-builtin validation** (eager, declaration-site check in collect pass)
11b. **Option `extends` validation** — replace the existing warning with actual validation: parent existence, visibility, circular detection
12. **Qualified name resolution** — `check_ident` returns `ModuleAlias` for aliases; `resolve_field` handles `ModuleAlias` arm for types/conditions/variants; `check_call` handles `FieldAccess` with alias LHS for function calls

### Phase 5: CLI Integration
13. **Multi-file load command parsing** — change `commands.rs` `load` branch to pass full tail (not just first token)
14. **Multi-file load + glob** — `glob` crate, multi-path `cmd_load`, `reload` with `Vec<PathBuf>`
15. **Diagnostic rendering** — store loaded sources on Runner, construct `MultiSourceMap`, update `cmd_errors` to use `MultiSourceMap::render()`

### Phase 6: Spec + Tests
16. **Update spec** — `spec/v0/02_scoping.ttrpg` with new grammar and semantics
17. **Integration tests** — multi-file fixtures exercising all edge cases

---

## Verification

### Core functionality
- Parse `use "X" as Y` and verify AST has alias
- Parse `Core.Character` as type, verify `TypeExpr::Qualified`
- Multi-file load: two files with different systems → merged Program has both
- Same-name system blocks across files merge correctly
- `load a.ttrpg b.ttrpg` → preserves explicit ordering (a first)
- `load *.ttrpg` → glob results sorted alphabetically
- `load /tmp//file.ttrpg` → `//` in path preserved (not treated as comment)
- `load *.nonexistent` → hard error "no files matched pattern"
- `load a.ttrpg *.nonexistent b.ttrpg` → hard error (fail-fast, not partial load)

### Global duplicate detection
- Two systems define `modifier` (function) → load-time error naming both files/lines
- Two systems define `Ability` (type) → error
- Two enums in different systems define variant `STR` → error (variant namespace collision)
- Same name in different namespaces (type `Foo` + function `Foo`) → **not** a conflict
- Duplicate declaration within same merged system → error

### Visibility enforcement
- System A uses System B: A can reference B's declarations
- System A does NOT use System C: A referencing C's declaration → "not visible" error
- Transitive: A uses B, B uses C, A references C → error (not transitive)

### Non-expression visibility enforcement
- Reaction trigger referencing non-imported event → "not visible" error (`check.rs:202`)
- Hook trigger referencing non-imported event → "not visible" error (same path)
- Suppress clause referencing non-imported event → "not visible" error (`check_modify.rs:278`)
- Modify clause targeting non-imported function → "not visible" error (`check_modify.rs:22`)
- Modify clause targeting an imported function → valid
- Suppress clause targeting an imported event → valid
- Type signature in function referencing non-imported type → "not visible" error (collect pass 1b)
- `let x: NonImportedType = ...` in function body → "not visible" error (`check_stmt.rs:33` via `resolve_type_visible`)

### Qualified access (all chains)
- `Core.modifier(10)` — function call through alias (`check_call` FieldAccess dispatch)
- `Core.Ability` — type name through alias (expression position → `Ty::EnumType`, NOT `Ty::Enum`)
- `Core.Ability.STR` — enum variant through alias (two-step: `ModuleAlias` → `EnumType` → variant)
- `Core.Prone` — condition name through alias → `Ty::Condition`
- `Core.nonexistent` → error naming the aliased system
- `UnknownAlias.foo` → "undefined variable" (not a known alias)
- `Core.Character` in type position → `TypeExpr::Qualified` desugared to `TypeExpr::Named("Character")` during resolution, before `collect()`
- `Core.Character` in type position within a function signature → works because desugar happens pre-collect

### Option extends
- `option X extends "Y"` where Y is in an imported system → valid
- `option X extends "Y"` where Y is in a non-imported system → "not imported" error
- `option X extends "Y"` where Y does not exist → "unknown option" error
- Circular extends (A→B→A) → error
- `option X extends "Y"` where Y is in the same system → valid (trivial case)

### Edge cases
- Circular use (A↔B) → both see each other, no error
- Self-import → warning
- Use with no system block → warning
- Local declaration shadows import → no error
- Alias collides with own declaration → error
- Alias collides with another alias → error
- Alias shadows imported declaration → error
- Alias shadows builtin function name → error
- Same `use "A" as X` in two files for same system → idempotent, no error
- `use "A" as X` + `use "B" as X` in different files for same system → error (different targets)

### Backward compatibility
- Single-file `load core.ttrpg` → works exactly as before
- `check(program)` without modules → no visibility restrictions
- REPL `eval` with no system context → everything visible
- All existing tests pass unchanged

### Diagnostic rendering
- **All `load` paths** (single- and multi-file): `MultiSourceMap` always constructed, `cmd_errors` displays diagnostics with `filename:line:col` and caret underlines. Existing single-file load tests that assert `[error] message` format must be updated to expect the new rich format.
- **REPL `eval` errors**: NOT stored in `self.diagnostics` — returned directly as `CliError::Message` from `eval()` (`runner.rs:207`). `cmd_errors` never displays eval errors. No change to eval error flow.
- Span rebasing: error in file 2 points to correct line (not offset by file 1's length)
- Span at exact EOF of file N correctly attributed to file N (not file N+1) — sentinel gap

### Fail-fast gating
- Duplicate declaration across systems → checker never runs, no secondary errors
- Parse error in one file → resolution still runs on other files' AST, but checker never runs
- Resolution warning (self-import) → checker still runs (warnings don't gate)

### Regressions to verify
- Existing `load /tmp//file.ttrpg` test passes (commands.rs:307)
- Existing `load /some/path // comment` test passes (commands.rs:316)
- All existing single-file tests pass unchanged
- Declaration order (reactions, options, hooks) is deterministic and matches file load order
- Alias collision introduced by cross-file system fragment union detected correctly

description:
File: crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_control.rs

Why this is a bug: check_expr_expecting carries expected-type hints to disambiguate variants and infer literals, but ExprKind::If and ExprKind::IfLet call into check_if/check_if_let which currently type-check branches via check_block/check_expr without threading any hint. This drops contextual type information for branch bodies. As a result, valid code can fail with ambiguity/type errors (for example, bare variants that require an expected enum hint, or branch literals that rely on expected element types).

Potential impact: Valid if/if let expressions in typed contexts (return positions, argument positions, etc.) can be rejected with false type errors, while equivalent match expressions type-check correctly because they do thread hints. This is a user-visible type-checking inconsistency.

Suggested fix: Add an optional expected-type hint parameter to check_if/check_if_let and check_else_branch_type, then check then/else blocks with check_block_with_tail_hint(..., hint) and nested else-if expressions with check_expr_expecting(..., hint) so branch inference/disambiguation uses context.
title: Bug: if/if let branches ignore expected type hints

close_reason: pipe/script mode now continues on error instead of exiting immediately, so diagnostics are reachable
description:
In pipe mode (run_pipe, main.rs:38) and script mode (run_script, main.rs:64), any command error calls process::exit(1) immediately. This means if `load` fails with parse/check errors, the subsequent `errors` command never runs — the user cannot see what went wrong.

The diagnostics ARE stored on the Runner (runner.rs:280), but the process exits before the next line of stdin/script is read.

Options:
- Don't exit on load failure in pipe/script mode — just print the error and continue
- Auto-print diagnostics on load failure (first N errors)
- Add a --keep-going flag for script mode
- In pipe mode, treat load failure as non-fatal (the Runner is still usable, just with no program loaded)
title: CLI: pipe/script mode exits on load failure, diagnostics unreachable

This likely needs changes in the interpreter's struct instantiation logic to check for and evaluate default expressions from the AST/Program when a field is not explicitly provided.

design:
## Design Decisions (2026-03-05)

### File organization
Split into two files: `osric_cleric_spells.ttrpg` (Bless/Curse, CLW/CLW-reverse, Hold Person) and `osric_mu_spells.ttrpg` (Sleep, Magic Missile, Fireball).

### Pattern per spell
1. `derive <spell>_def() -> SpellDef` — static data
2. `function resolve_<spell>(...)` — effect resolution
3. Per-spell integration test in `crates/ttrpg_interp/tests/`

### New conditions
- `Blessed` (+1 attack, +5% morale) — 6 rounds duration
- `Cursed` (-1 attack, -5% morale) — 6 rounds duration
Hold Person reuses `Paralyzed`, Sleep reuses `Sleeping`.

### Caster level derive
`caster_level(char, spell_class) -> int` in `osric_class.ttrpg`.

### Reverse spells
Implement Curse and Cause Light Wounds alongside their forward versions.

### Damage events
Spell damage uses existing `Damaged(target, attacker, amount)` event to trigger spell interruption hooks.

### Sleep
Full HD-category fidelity (per-category rolls, weakest-first processing). Separate bead due to complexity.

### Implementation order
Bless/Curse → CLW/CLW-rev → Magic Missile → Fireball → Hold Person → Sleep
title: Implement core combat spells (Bless, CLW, Hold Person, Sleep, Magic Missile, Fireball)

close_reason: Added missing check_name_visible calls for method-style action calls and emit statements
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_call.rs

Why this is a bug: In check_call, the ExprKind::FieldAccess path treats entity-dot-action calls as action calls by looking up the field in env.lookup_fn and returning early, but it never calls check_name_visible for the action name. Direct calls do enforce visibility via check_name_visible with Namespace::Function. This creates inconsistent semantics and allows calling non-visible actions via method syntax.

Potential impact: Code that should fail module visibility/import checks can type-check successfully when rewritten as entity.action(...), bypassing encapsulation boundaries and incorrectly accepting invalid programs.

Suggested fix: In the method-style action branch, run the same non-builtin visibility check as direct calls (for example, check_name_visible(field, Namespace::Function, span)) before validating arguments and return type.
title: Bug: Method-style action calls bypass visibility checks

description: Remaining OSRIC spells such as Dispel Magic, Anti-Magic Shell, Globe/Minor Globe of Invulnerability, Remove Curse, and parts of Prismatic Sphere need a reusable way to identify active magical effects, know their source spell/caster level, and suppress or remove them generically. Current DSL access to ActiveCondition exposes only name/duration/id, and OSRIC spell effects are not tagged with spell provenance or unified interception hooks. Add the runtime/model support needed for generic dispel and anti-magic behavior.
title: Add magical-effect metadata and interception for dispel / anti-magic spells
