close_reason: all steps complete
description:
Module loading currently depends on explicit file lists or glob expansion at the CLI boundary, while `use "System"` only affects symbol visibility after parsing. That split leaks into several places: REPL `load` requires enumerating every file, bundled data crates manually maintain file inventories, and tests hand-build source vectors.

This epic tracks improving source discovery so users can load an entrypoint or package and let the loader resolve the rest in a principled way. The work should preserve existing module visibility semantics where appropriate, but make source loading a first-class concept with better diagnostics and less duplicated inventory maintenance.
title: Improve module/source loading ergonomics

close_reason: Implemented import directive with transitive source resolution
description:
Introduce a source-resolution layer ahead of `parse_multi` so callers can load a single entry file and have transitive source dependencies resolved automatically.

Scope:
- Add an `import "path.ttrpg"` directive for file-level source loading, distinct from `use "System"` which remains purely symbol visibility. The source resolver follows `import` edges to build the file graph transitively from an entrypoint.
- `import` appears at file top-level (alongside `use`) and takes a relative path to another .ttrpg file.
- `use "System"` semantics are unchanged: symbol visibility / namespace imports only.
- Canonicalize paths, dedupe files, detect cycles, and produce a stable load order.
- Expose the resolver through the CLI `load` path and any shared loader APIs.

Why:
- Today `crates/ttrpg_cli/src/runner/load.rs` only tokenizes explicit paths and expands globs.
- `crates/ttrpg_parser/src/resolve.rs` validates imports only after the full source set is already loaded.
- Users should be able to load an entrypoint rather than hand-maintain a file list.
- Separating `import` (file loading) from `use` (symbol visibility) makes both concepts explicit and avoids overloading `use` with resolution duties.
title: Add source resolver for entrypoint-based loading

close_reason: Added source_files and decl_spans to SystemInfo, file_id to FileSystemInfo. Cross-system collision diagnostics now use real spans with 'also defined in' pointers. Unknown system errors include importing system name. 5 new tests.
description:
Extend module-loading metadata so systems and imports retain enough source-origin information to explain where a system came from and why it could not be loaded.

Scope:
- Record which file(s) define each system and which source edge introduced them.
- Distinguish source-loading relationships from semantic `use` visibility where needed.
- Improve diagnostics for missing systems/files to report the import chain or source path that led to the failure.
- Surface clearer errors for duplicate system definitions, shadowing, and no-effect imports in multi-file loads.

Why:
- `ModuleMap` and `SystemInfo` currently know names and semantic imports, but not source provenance.
- Once source loading is more automatic, diagnostics need to explain loader decisions instead of only saying a system is unknown.
title: Record source origins in module metadata and improve missing-module diagnostics

close_reason: Implemented ttrpg.toml manifest format with entries, bundles, and target resolution. CLI supports load <pkg> and load <pkg>:<target>. Manifests created for OSE and OSRIC.
description:
Add a package-level manifest (`ttrpg.toml`) for rule content so callers can load a package or named target instead of relying on raw globs or handwritten file lists.

## Manifest format (v1)

```toml
manifest_version = 1

[package]
name = "ose"
default_target = "full"

[entries.core]
path = "ose_core.ttrpg"

[entries.combat]
path = "ose_combat.ttrpg"

[bundles.full]
entries = ["core", "class", "combat", "equipment", "magic", "spells"]

[bundles.chargen]
entries = ["core", "class", "chargen", "equipment"]
```

## Terminology

- **entry**: A single root source file. The source resolver follows its `import` edges transitively to build the file graph.
- **bundle**: A curated composition of multiple entries. Replaces handwritten source vectors and most globs.
- **target**: Either an entry or a bundle. The unit of CLI loading.
- **default_target**: What `ttrpg load <package>` resolves to (usually a bundle representing the full usable ruleset).

## CLI semantics

- `ttrpg load ose` → load package default target (usually `full` bundle)
- `ttrpg load ose:core` → load single entry
- `ttrpg load ose:chargen` → load named bundle

## Design principles

- The manifest describes package identity, entrypoints, and named bundles only.
- The file graph lives in source files via `import` directives, not in the manifest.
- The manifest should not list every transitive file; the resolver follows imports from entry files.
- `default_target` should be the most useful complete experience, not the minimal seed.

## Deferred to later versions

- Inter-package `[dependencies]` (local path or otherwise)
- Explicit system-name-to-file maps
- Per-file metadata blocks
- Glob patterns inside the manifest
- Publish/registry semantics
- Version constraints
title: Add package manifest and named bundle entrypoints for rule modules

close_reason: Replaced hand-coded source inventories with manifest-driven generation. OSE crates use build.rs to read ttrpg.toml and generate FILES arrays. OSRIC test helper reads manifest at runtime via load_bundle_sources().

description: In crates/ttrpg_checker/src/collect.rs:58-62, collect_inner calls pass_1b separately for each system. Inside pass_1b (crates/ttrpg_checker/src/collect.rs:252), processed_types is local to one call. If two systems declare the same type name, pass_1a reports a duplicate (lines 228-239) and keeps the first stub, but the second system declaration is still processed in a later pass_1b call and writes into the same existing entry via get_mut in collectors (for example lines 431-433, 458-460, 541-544, 619-622). close_reason: Audit complete. Design note written with 3 tiers of candidates (6 extractions into ttrpg_interp/ttrpg_parser, 8 non-extractions staying in CLI). 7 follow-up beads created for individual extraction tasks.
description:
The CLI currently acts as a full host implementation on top of ttrpg_interp. It contains several integration patterns and conveniences that may be broadly useful to future hosts, but today they live in ttrpg_cli-specific code. We should examine the CLI host layer and decide which concepts belong in ttrpg_interp as reusable integration primitives versus which should remain CLI-only behavior.

Motivation:
- Avoid letting the CLI become the de facto host API.
- Keep the runtime contract defined by ttrpg_interp rather than by host-specific workarounds or conveniences.
- Make future non-CLI and non-Rust hosts easier by promoting general integration concepts into the core crate when appropriate.

Audit targets:
- RefCellState aliasing/borrow-management pattern around GameState and StateProvider.
- Mutation arithmetic and clamping logic currently handled in the CLI host.
- Prompt fallback behavior (queued values, suggest/default handling, interactive stdin behavior).
- Dice queue / seeded RNG host behaviors.
- Effect logging, entity naming, and formatting conventions that may or may not belong in core.
- Any other Layer 1 hosting patterns duplicated from or adjacent to StateAdapter behavior.

Questions to answer:
- Which CLI patterns are generally reusable host abstractions?
- Which should move into ttrpg_interp as public helpers or integration utilities?
- Which are intentionally CLI-specific and should stay out of the runtime contract?
- Are there cases where CLI behavior has implicitly shaped runtime semantics or documentation in undesirable ways?

Deliverable:
- A short design note or issue breakdown identifying candidate extractions into ttrpg_interp, recommended non-extractions, and any follow-up refactor beads needed.
design:
# Audit: CLI Host Integration Patterns

## Architecture Summary

ttrpg_interp provides three core traits (StateProvider, WritableState, EffectHandler) plus a reference GameState implementation. The CLI builds a substantial host layer on top. Several patterns are generic enough to extract, while others are intentionally CLI-specific.

## Candidate Extractions

### Tier 1: Move into ttrpg_interp (or new ttrpg_host crate)

**1. RefCellState wrapper** (effects.rs:44-90)
- Generic StateProvider impl over &RefCell<GameState>
- Every host using RefCell will re-implement this identically
- Zero CLI dependencies; pure delegation
- Recommendation: Move to ttrpg_interp next to GameState

**2. TrackedInterpreter RAII** (runner/mod.rs:613-648)
- Seeds Interpreter invocation counter from GameState, persists on drop
- Any host persisting state across evaluations needs this
- Recommendation: Move to ttrpg_interp as InterpreterSession or similar

**3. compute_field_value / clamping detection** (effects.rs:249-308)
- Reads old value, applies op (+= -= =), clamps, detects clamping
- Core mutation arithmetic that any host applying MutateField needs
- Currently duplicates logic that could live near GameState.write_field
- Recommendation: Move to ttrpg_interp as a utility on MutateField or GameState

**4. Source resolution** (source_resolve.rs, 294 lines)
- BFS import traversal from entrypoint files
- No CLI dependencies (uses ttrpg_parser::extract_imports)
- Already reusable; just happens to live in CLI
- Recommendation: Move to ttrpg_parser (closest to import parsing)

**5. Manifest/package resolution** (manifest.rs, runner/load.rs)
- TOML manifest parsing and target resolution
- No CLI dependencies beyond file I/O
- Recommendation: New shared location (ttrpg_parser or standalone)

### Tier 2: Provide as optional utilities

**6. Dice queue + RNG handler** (effects.rs:164-189)
- VecDeque<i64> front-pop per die, validate range, fallback to StdRng
- Essential for deterministic testing in any host
- Recommendation: Provide as QueuedDiceProvider in ttrpg_interp; hosts can compose into their EffectHandler

**7. Prompt queue + fallback** (effects.rs:191-247)
- Queue → interactive callback → suggest → default hierarchy
- Reusable pattern but interactive callback is host-specific
- Recommendation: Provide PromptResolver trait + QueuedPromptProvider utility

**8. Entity handle registry** (runner/mod.rs handle maps)
- Bidirectional String↔EntityRef mapping
- Every user-facing host needs human-readable entity names
- Recommendation: Provide HandleRegistry utility in ttrpg_interp

### Tier 3: Consider for new ttrpg_host crate

If Tier 1+2 extractions bloat ttrpg_interp's API surface too much, a separate ttrpg_host crate could house:
- RefCellState, TrackedInterpreter, HandleRegistry
- QueuedDiceProvider, QueuedPromptProvider
- compute_field_value / mutation helpers
- Source resolution and manifest loading

This keeps ttrpg_interp focused on the interpreter core while providing a "batteries included" host integration layer.

## Recommended Non-Extractions (Stay in CLI)

**1. REPL subsystem** — reedline, completions, highlighting, validation
- Deeply CLI-specific; no other host wants terminal line editing
- Correct location

**2. CLI command grammar** (commands.rs, 978 lines)
- 50+ text commands (spawn, set, destroy, eval, assert, etc.)
- This IS the CLI's value proposition; not a general host concern

**3. Interactive stdin prompts** — raw mode toggling, terminal I/O
- Only relevant for terminal hosts

**4. Output formatting** (format.rs) — colored output, unit suffixes
- Presentation layer; hosts will have their own rendering

**5. Assert commands** (runner/assert.rs) — testing DSL
- CLI-specific testing convenience

**6. Heredoc/continuation state machine** — multi-line input accumulation
- Terminal interaction pattern only

**7. Quiet mode / interactive mode flags** — CLI UX concerns

**8. Coverage tracking** — CLI-specific reporting integration

## Cases Where CLI Shaped Runtime Semantics

1. **Prompt fallback order**: The CLI's queue→suggest→default hierarchy has become the de facto prompt contract. Other hosts may want different strategies. Extracting PromptResolver as a trait formalizes this.

2. **Clamping as logging concern**: Clamping detection lives in CLI but the bounds info comes from the interpreter. The interpreter should probably provide a utility to compute clamped results rather than making each host re-derive the arithmetic.

3. **Entity naming**: The opaque EntityRef design is correct, but the lack of any standard handle registry means every host re-invents bidirectional maps. A standard utility would codify the pattern without coupling it to the runtime.

## Recommended Follow-up Beads

1. Extract RefCellState + TrackedInterpreter into ttrpg_interp
2. Extract compute_field_value / mutation arithmetic into ttrpg_interp
3. Move source_resolve into ttrpg_parser
4. Move manifest handling to shared location
5. Add QueuedDiceProvider + QueuedPromptProvider utilities
6. Add HandleRegistry utility
7. Evaluate whether a ttrpg_host crate is warranted after items 1-6
title: Audit ttrpg_cli host integration for reusable runtime patterns

close_reason: Implemented: compute_common_params now unifies entity-typed params with different specific types to AnyEntity instead of dropping them. This allows conditions with 'on bearer: entity' to use tag selector bindings that match functions with different entity-typed params (e.g., attacker: Character and attacker: Monster).
description: If tag selector bindings allowed attacker: entity (the polymorphic any-entity alias), a single modify clause could match both Character and Monster params. In osric_conditions.ttrpg, conditions like Fleeing and Surprised want to suppress all attacks by bearer regardless of whether the matched function takes Character or Monster as attacker. The condition body only uses result field overrides (result.outcome = AttackOutcome.Miss, result.damage = 0) which are entity-type-agnostic. This is an alternative or complement to the skip-mismatched-types approach in the other issue.
title: Allow entity keyword in tag selector bindings for polymorphic matching

description: Prompts currently require the host to respond via PromptResult. Add an optional 'default' clause that provides fallback logic when the host returns Response::UseDefault (or a new response variant). This enables prompts to self-resolve in automated contexts (tests, simulations) while still deferring to the host for interactive play. Syntax: prompt foo(...) -> T { hint: '...', suggest: x, default { expr } }. The default body executes in the same scope as suggest, with access to bound parameters. Motivating use case: unarmed combat fend-off decisions where the default is 'all eligible armed defenders attempt to fend off'.
title: Add default body to prompts for auto-resolution

close_reason: Fixed all 4 alias/shadow resolution bugs
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_has.rs

Why this is a bug: In check_has(), alias shadow validation only checks env.lookup_fields(entity) and env.lookup_optional_group(entity, alias). For entities with required include groups, flattened fields are exposed via env.lookup_flattened_field(entity, field) during field resolution, but check_has does not guard against alias collisions with those flattened names. As a result, a valid field like m.ac (from an included group) can be silently reinterpreted as an alias to a different optional group inside a has-guarded scope.

Potential impact: Incorrect name resolution and type checking in valid programs. Accesses to flattened included fields can change meaning or fail unexpectedly inside if entity has Group as alias blocks, producing misleading diagnostics and incorrect behavior for group-aware logic.

Suggested fix: In check_has alias validation for Ty::Entity, also check env.lookup_flattened_field(entity_name, alias_name) and emit the same shadowing error when present.
title: Bug: has-alias shadow check misses flattened included fields

close_reason: Closed
description: Bug in crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_expr.rs lines 1547-1555: check_append_call checks the second argument with check_expr before validating against list element type. When list element type is known, ambiguous bare enum variants in the appended element are not disambiguated and valid code is rejected. Repro shape: append(list<DamageType>, fire) where fire is shared by multiple enums should use DamageType as hint but currently reports ambiguity.
notes: Part of type-hint batch — fix with tdsl-1cyf
title: append element argument ignores list element type hint

close_reason: Implemented and verified
description: OSRIC rangers receive 2d8 hit points at 1st level and a total of 11 hit dice before switching to fixed +2 hp per level. The DSL currently models rangers as 1d8 + 1 at level 1 via bonus_first_hd, and class_def sets max_hit_dice to 10, so ranger HP is understated both at character creation and across later advancement. Reference: OSRIC PG §1.3.9.1 and Table 1.3.9.4a. Affected files: osric/osric_class.ttrpg, osric/osric_chargen.ttrpg, osric/osric_levelup.ttrpg.
title: Fix ranger hit-die progression in chargen and level-up

close_reason: Migrated dnd5e_expanded to use map<int, resource(0..9)> for spell_slots. Replaced 9 spell_slots_N fields with single resource-valued map. Replaced 9-branch matches in CureWounds and Fireball with spell_slots[slot_level] -= 1. Updated pain point comments and summary. File loads and type-checks cleanly.
description:
Spell slots in 5e are a resource indexed by level (1-9). The DSL forces 9 separate fields:
    spell_slots_1: int = 0
    spell_slots_2: int = 0
    ...
    spell_slots_9: int = 0

Ideally: `spell_slots: map<int, resource(0..max_slots)>`
But resource types cannot be values in a map. There is also no way to use a runtime int to select a field (no computed field access like `entity.spell_slots[level]`).
title: Language: indexed resources / map of resources

description: Monster-only. Base morale = 50% + 5% per hit die. Roll d100 vs modified threshold. Modifiers table: +5% per friend killed/surrendered/fled, +5% at 25% casualties, +10% numerical inferiority, +15% at 50% casualties, +20% outnumbered 2:1, +25% leader defeated, -5% per foe killed, -5% at 25% enemy casualties, -10% numerical superiority, -15% at 50% enemy casualties. Max 2 checks per battle. Fail by 25% = retreat, fail by 51% = surrender. Ref: PG p100.
title: Implement morale check mechanic

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_ast/src/span.rs

Why this is a bug: Span::merge relies on debug_assert_eq!(self.file.0, other.file.0), which is compiled out in release builds. When called with spans from different files, it silently returns a merged span that keeps self.file but combines start/end from both spans, producing an invalid source location.

Potential impact: Incorrect source mapping in diagnostics or downstream logic, with hard-to-trace misattribution of errors to the wrong file.

Suggested fix: Enforce same-file merging in all builds (e.g., assert!/panic, Result-returning API, or explicit cross-file handling) instead of debug_assert-only validation.
title: Bug: Span::merge allows cross-file merge in release builds

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check.rs

Why this is a bug: check_condition and check_event type-check parameter defaults inside a fresh scope but never bind previously declared parameters before checking later defaults. This differs from bind_params (used for functions/actions/prompts), which binds params incrementally so defaults like b: int = a are valid. As written, valid condition/event defaults that reference earlier params are incorrectly reported as undefined/type errors.

Potential impact: Valid DSL programs are rejected during checking when condition/event defaults depend on prior parameters, despite runtime argument binding supporting this pattern.

Suggested fix: Reuse bind_params-style incremental binding for condition/event default validation (or explicitly bind prior params in order while checking defaults), so earlier parameters are in scope for later default expressions.
title: Bug: Condition/event default params cannot reference earlier params

close_reason: Implemented condition composition via extends keyword. Conditions can now inherit modify/suppress clauses from parents with multi-parent support, cycle detection, and diamond dedup.
description:
Many 5e conditions include effects of other conditions:
- Stunned includes Incapacitated (can't take actions/reactions)
- Unconscious includes Prone + Incapacitated
- Paralyzed includes Incapacitated + auto-fail STR/DEX saves

Currently all effects must be duplicated in each condition. Would like:
    condition Stunned extends Incapacitated on bearer: Character { ... }
or:
    condition Stunned on bearer: Character {
        include Incapacitated
        ...additional effects...
    }
title: Language: condition composition / inheritance

close_reason: Fixed with regression tests
description: In crates/ttrpg_checker/src/scope.rs lines 181-188, is_group_narrowed scans narrowed_groups across all active scopes and returns true on any matching key, but it does not account for identifier shadowing. If an outer binding/path like actor is narrowed, then an inner scope binds a new actor, checks for the inner actor can incorrectly inherit the outer proof. This can allow optional-group field access or with-constrained calls to type-check without a valid has/with proof for the current binding. Suggested fix: stop lookup when a nearer scope binds the root identifier, or track narrowings by binding identity instead of raw string keys.
title: ScopeStack::is_group_narrowed leaks optional-group proofs across shadowed bindings

OSRIC 3.0 section 1.6.2.11 says casting can continue into next round and casters receive no dexterity AC bonus while casting. Update initiative/casting model to preserve cross-round timing and apply no-DEX-AC while casting.
title: Spell casting initiative timing loses cross-round behavior and DEX AC penalty

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_cli/src/effects.rs

Why this is a bug: roll_dice normalizes sides with (group.sides as i64).max(1), so d0 is treated as 1..=1. roll_dice_from_queue validates queued values against 1..=group.sides directly, so with sides == 0 it rejects all queued values and vetoes.

Potential impact: Queued deterministic rolls can fail while non-queued rolls succeed for the same expression, causing inconsistent runtime behavior.

Suggested fix: Normalize sides in roll_dice_from_queue the same way as roll_dice before validation and fallback RNG.
title: Bug: Queue dice validation diverges from normal d0 handling

close_reason: FALSE POSITIVE: checker validates table argument arity at compile time; runtime zip truncation is safe
description: In crates/ttrpg_interp/src/call.rs lines 660-672, dispatch_table_with_values forwards pre-evaluated args without validating arity. In dispatch_table_core at line 700, entry keys are compared with arg values using zip, which truncates to the shorter side. Extra args are ignored and missing args leave trailing keys unchecked, so an entry can be treated as a match when full-arity matching should fail. This can return incorrect table results instead of an arity error for pre-evaluated table calls.
title: dispatch_table_with_values can match wrong entries when arg count is wrong

close_reason: Already done — the grammar was updated in commit 67095bb (Spec audit: grammar conformance) to make event body braces optional: event_decl = "event" IDENT "(" params ")" ("{" field_defs "}")? term
description: The canonical grammar says event_decl = "event" IDENT "(" params ")" "{" field_def* "}", requiring braces. The parser accepts events without braces when the body is empty (e.g. 'event Foo(target: Character)' without '{}'). The full example uses both forms — line 388 has '{}' and line 552 omits it. This is a harmless convenience. Update the spec grammar to make braces optional: event_decl = "event" IDENT "(" params ")" ("{" field_defs "}")?.
title: Update spec: event body braces optional when empty

close_reason: Completed
description: Initiative spell resolution bypasses the main CastSpell action. It begins casting and later resolves the spell directly, which skips memorized-slot consumption and other CastSpell gating, and it can still complete a spell after the caster has taken damage because the queued action is resolved even after the casting condition is removed. OSRIC requires declared spells to be memorized and lost when interrupted before taking effect. Reference: OSRIC PG §1.8.1 and §1.6.2.11. Affected files: osric/osric_initiative.ttrpg, osric/osric_spells.ttrpg.
title: Honor memorization and interruption in initiative spellcasting

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_checker/src/collect.rs

Why this is a bug: In validate_option_extends(), cycle detection notices revisits via visited, but the error-chain construction assumes the cycle closes back to start_name. For a graph like A->B->C->B, starting from A detects a cycle at B; the chain builder then walks A->B->C->B->... and never reaches A, so the while-loop never terminates.

Potential impact: The checker can hang indefinitely on invalid option-extends input, blocking diagnostics and potentially wedging CLI/editor workflows.

Suggested fix: When building the cycle message, stop when revisiting any node (or when revisiting the detected cycle node) instead of requiring parent == start_name; alternatively track predecessor/path during detection and reconstruct only the actual cycle segment.
title: Bug: Option extends cycle error builder can loop forever

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_parser/src/pattern.rs

Why this is a bug: In parse_pattern, the raw_name == 'some' recovery branch runs before qualified-pattern handling. Inputs like some.Variant or some.Variant(x) are valid qualified patterns when a type or alias is named some, but they are misparsed as bare some and converted to PatternKind::Some(_) with a diagnostic.

Potential impact: Valid match, if-let, and for patterns become unparsable or are parsed incorrectly when the leading type or alias identifier is some, causing false errors and broken behavior in affected code.

Suggested fix: Only apply bare-some recovery when the next token is not Dot; allow the existing qualified-pattern path (IDENT.DOT.IDENT...) to run first for some.* forms.
title: Bug: Pattern parser rejects qualified names starting with 'some'

description: Lose one complete level of experience (placed at beginning XP of new lower level). Multi-class: highest level lost, or random if tied. Death below level 1. Ref: PG p100.
design:
## Level Drain Design

### Rules (§1.6.7, §1.8.2.7)
- Lose one level of experience, XP set to beginning of new lower level
- Multi/dual-class: drain highest level; random (roll) if tied
- Below level 1 = death (PCs)
- **Restorable** via Restoration (Cleric 7): restores one lost level, XP to start of restored level
- Restoration time window: 1 day per caster's cleric level. Miss it and the level is gone permanently.
- Drain and restore are symmetric — no need to track old XP, just the event

### Data Model (in osric_core.ttrpg)

```
struct DrainEvent {
    class: Class         // which class was drained
    original_level: int  // level before this drain
    timestamp: int       // game-time in segments when drain occurred
}
```

Character entity gets: `drain_history: list<DrainEvent>`

### Key Decisions
- **Randomness**: use roll() for tied-class selection; testable via roll specs
- **One drain at a time**: caller loops for multi-drain undead
- **Restore order**: oldest applicable drain first (FIFO) — already punishing enough
- **Time unit**: segments (existing helpers convert days/turns/rounds)
- **HP recalc**: deferred to tdsl-1xcs
- **NPC rules**: deferred to tdsl-cnww

### Derives (in osric/osric_level_drain.ttrpg)
1. highest_class_level(char) -> int
2. drain_candidate_classes(char) -> list<Class>
3. drain_target_class(char) -> Class  // rolls if tied
4. xp_after_drain(class, current_level) -> int
5. apply_level_drain(classes: list<ClassLevel>, target: Class) -> list<ClassLevel>
6. is_killed_by_drain(level: int) -> bool
7. record_drain_event(char, target_class, current_time) -> list<DrainEvent>
8. restoration_eligible(drain: DrainEvent, current_time: int, caster_cleric_level: int) -> bool
9. apply_restoration(char, current_time, caster_cleric_level) -> list<ClassLevel>  // restores oldest eligible
10. remove_oldest_eligible_drain(drain_history, current_time, caster_cleric_level) -> list<DrainEvent>

### Files
- osric/osric_core.ttrpg — DrainEvent struct, drain_history on Character
- osric/osric_level_drain.ttrpg — all derives
- crates/ttrpg_interp/tests/osric_level_drain_integration.rs — tests
title: Implement level drain mechanic

description: Enable creating entities directly in DSL code using the same syntax as struct literals (e.g., Monster { name: "Ogre", ... }), removing the need for host-only spawn. design:
## Design: DSL-Side Entity Construction

### Motivation
Entity creation is currently host-only (CLI spawn command or Rust GameState::add_entity()). To build bestiaries, character factories, or any DSL-side entity creation, we need entity construction as a DSL expression.

### Syntax
Use the same syntax as struct literals — no new keyword. The checker already knows which names are entities vs structs.

```ttrpg
let ogre = Monster {
    name: "Ogre",
    hit_dice: 4d8+1,
    max_hp: 20,
    ac: 5,
    attacks: [MonsterAttack { name: "Club", damage: 1d10 }],
    EquipmentSlots {
        wielded_main: some(WieldedItem.Melee(MeleeWeapon.Club)),
    }
}
```

### Disambiguation
Inside the braces, the parser distinguishes:
- `name: expr` → field init (StructFieldInit)
- `Name { ... }` → group init (GroupInit) — PascalCase ident followed by `{`

### AST Changes
Add `groups: Vec<GroupInit>` to `ExprKind::StructLit` and a new `GroupInit` struct:
```rust
pub struct GroupInit {
    pub name: Name,
    pub fields: Vec<StructFieldInit>,
    pub span: Span,
}
```

### Checker Changes
- Remove the DeclInfo::Entity error in check_struct_lit
- For entities: validate base fields + inline groups against entity schema
- Enforce entity construction only in mutating contexts (function/action/reaction/hook)
- Return Ty::Entity(Name)

### Interpreter Changes
- When StructLit name resolves to entity type:
  - Evaluate fields + fill defaults
  - Call state.add_entity() to register
  - Process inline groups (evaluate + write as fields)
  - Auto-materialize required include groups with defaults
  - Initialize resource fields
  - Return Value::Entity(entity_ref)

### Context Restriction
Entity construction mutates GameState, so only allowed in: function, action, reaction, hook. Not in: derive, mechanic, table, const. The checker already tracks mutability context.

### Key Files
- AST: crates/ttrpg_ast/src/ast.rs (StructLit variant, new GroupInit)
- Parser: crates/ttrpg_parser/src/expr.rs (parse_struct_lit, parse_struct_field_init)
- Checker: crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_literal.rs (check_struct_lit)
- Interpreter: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/eval/dispatch.rs (StructLit eval)
- CLI spawn reference: crates/ttrpg_cli/src/runner/mutation.rs (existing spawn logic to mirror)
title: DSL-side entity construction via struct literal syntax

description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/builtins.rs

Why this is a bug: In builtin_remove_condition(), the ActiveCondition path reads id as Value::Int and converts it with a direct cast to u64. For negative ids this performs two's-complement wrapping, producing a huge positive u64 instead of rejecting invalid input.

Potential impact: A script can pass an ActiveCondition with id less than 0 and trigger RemoveCondition with an unintended large id, causing silent failure to remove the intended condition or incorrect host-side behavior if ids are interpreted directly.

Suggested fix: Validate id is non-negative before conversion and use checked conversion (for example u64::try_from(id)), returning RuntimeError on invalid values.
title: Bug: remove_condition wraps negative ActiveCondition id to u64

description: No way to automatically react when a resource crosses a boundary. Currently threshold checks are manual in every action that modifies HP (TakeDamage checks hp<=0). If HP is modified any other way (healing overflow, direct assignment, level drain), checks are missed. Two design options: (A) on-threshold syntax on entity resource fields: 'on hp <= 0 { ... }', or (B) auto-emit ResourceChanged event whenever interpreter mutates a resource, letting hooks react. Option B is lighter-weight and more composable. Blocks: auto-unconscious at 0 HP, auto-death at -10 HP, exhaustion thresholds, spell slot depletion triggers.
design:
## Design: restricted field modifier

### Summary
Add a `restricted` keyword modifier on entity/group fields that prevents mutation from outside the declaring module (system block). Read access is always allowed. This enforces encapsulation — resource mutations must go through actions exported by the declaring module.

### Syntax
```
entity Character {
    restricted HP: resource(0..=max_HP)
    restricted spell_slots: map<int, resource(0..=9)>
    name: string  // unrestricted, anyone can mutate
}

group HasHP {
    restricted hp: resource(0..=max_hp)
    max_hp: int
}
```

### Semantics
- `restricted` is opt-in per field (default: unrestricted)
- Applies to any entity or group field, not just resources
- **Declaring module** = the `system "Name" { ... }` block containing the field declaration
- Multiple `system "Name"` blocks with same name merge — all share write access
- Read access is always allowed from any module
- Assignment (`=`, `+=`, `-=`) to a restricted field is a **checker error** if the assignment statement is outside the declaring module

### Exceptions
- `grant entity.Group { field_inits }` is allowed cross-module (initialization, not ongoing mutation)
- `revoke entity.Group` is allowed cross-module (removes entire group, not field-level mutation)
- REPL direct assignment (`set`) bypasses restriction (debug tool)

### Module ownership for groups
If system A declares a group with a restricted field, and system B includes that group on an entity, **system A owns the restriction**. System B must call actions from system A to mutate the field. This is intentional: the group author controls the mutation contract.

### Error message
```
error: cannot mutate restricted field `HP` outside declaring module
  --> spells.tdsl:42:9
   |
42 |     target.HP = 0
   |     ^^^^^^^^^^^^ mutation not allowed here
   |
   = note: `HP` is declared as restricted in system "Core Combat"
   = help: use an action from "Core Combat" to modify this field
```

### Audit result
No cross-module mutations exist in the current codebase. This feature is forward-looking protection with zero migration burden.
title: restricted fields

Impact: user-declared types named Position or Condition are present in env.types but cannot be referenced by type annotations, causing unreachable declarations and confusing type mismatches.

description:
In crates/ttrpg_parser/src/decl.rs, parse_with_groups (lines 294-314) consumes commas as group separators but only stops when lookahead is IDENT followed by Colon (lines 305-308). It does not stop on Comma followed by RParen.

When parsing a parameter like: derive f(actor: Character with Spellcasting,) -> int { ... }
parse_with_groups consumes the trailing comma at line 310, then calls expect_ident() at line 311 and fails on RParen.

This conflicts with parse_params trailing-comma support in the same file (parse_params lines 253-266, especially lines 257-260), so trailing commas work for normal params but fail when with constraints are present.

Impact: valid parameter lists with trailing commas become parse errors for declarations that use parse_params whenever a parameter uses with constraints.
title: Parser rejects trailing comma after parameter with-groups

Affected files: osric/osric_inventory.ttrpg, osric/osric_xp.ttrpg, osric/osric_potions.ttrpg, osric/osric_scrolls.ttrpg.

close_reason: Implemented optional EquipmentSlots on Monster, widened equipment derives to entity, renamed natural attack actions to MonsterMeleeAttack/MonsterMissileAttack, added armed weapon combat mechanics and actions for Monster.
description: wielded_melee(), wielded_missile(), wielded_off_melee(), armor_movement_cap() and other equipment functions in osric_equipment.ttrpg only accept Character. Intelligent humanoid monsters that wield weapons cannot use these derives, blocking monster weapon combat through the standard pipeline.
design:
## Implementation Plan

**Core insight:** `MeleeAttack` = 'attack with wielded weapon' for any entity. `MonsterMeleeAttack` = 'attack with a natural/innate MonsterAttack entry.'

### Action naming:
- `MeleeAttack on Character (target: entity)` — existing, unchanged
- `MeleeAttack on Monster (target: entity)` — NEW, reads weapon from EquipmentSlots
- `MissileAttack on Character (target: entity, distance: Feet)` — existing, unchanged
- `MissileAttack on Monster (target: entity, distance: Feet)` — NEW, reads weapon from EquipmentSlots
- `MonsterMeleeAttack on Monster (target: entity, attack: MonsterAttack)` — RENAMED from old MeleeAttack
- `MonsterMissileAttack on Monster (target: entity, attack: MonsterAttack, distance: Feet)` — RENAMED from old MissileAttack

### Changes by file:
1. `osric_core.ttrpg` — Add `optional EquipmentSlots` to Monster
2. `osric_equipment.ttrpg` — Widen wielded_melee, wielded_missile, wielded_off_melee, armor_movement_cap to entity with is/has guards
3. `osric_combat.ttrpg` — Rename existing Monster actions to MonsterMeleeAttack/MonsterMissileAttack; add resolve_armed_melee/missile_attack mechanics; add new MeleeAttack/MissileAttack on Monster using wielded weapons
4. Tests — Rename MeleeAttack(monster,...,MonsterAttack) calls to MonsterMeleeAttack; add armed-monster test cases
5. Keep equip/unequip functions Character-only (monsters get equipment at spawn time)

### Design decisions:
- No ability scores on monsters — armed monsters use monster_bthb (hit dice) for to-hit, weapon base damage with no STR modifier
- Compatible with future tdsl-7mn0 (initiative widening to entity) — resolve_action can call combatant.MeleeAttack(target) uniformly
title: Equipment/wielding functions are Character-only

close_reason: Refactored desugar_qualified_types to derive alias maps from validated module_map.systems[*].imports instead of rebuilding from raw file_systems use_decls
description: In resolve_modules(), Step 4 validates and deduplicates imports into module_map.systems[*].imports, but Step 6 (desugar_qualified_types) rebuilds alias maps from raw file_systems/use_decls. This can reintroduce invalid or conflicting aliases (for example duplicate aliases with different targets) and produce inconsistent follow-up diagnostics or wrong alias-to-system resolution during TypeExpr::Qualified desugaring. Refactor desugaring to derive aliases from validated imports in module_map (or otherwise skip invalid aliases captured during validation), so later phases consume the same canonical import state.
title: crates/ttrpg_parser/src/resolve.rs: desugaring uses raw file aliases instead of validated imports

close_reason: Design choice: defensive hardening only — no current code path creates malformed spans
description: In crates/ttrpg_ast/src/diagnostic.rs, span math is unchecked in render_diagnostic (lines 94-95 and 122). local_start = diag.span.start - base_offset / local_end = diag.span.end - base_offset and later local_end - local_start can underflow when a diagnostic has end < start (or when offsets are otherwise inconsistent). In debug builds this can panic; in release it can wrap to a huge usize and drive "^".repeat(caret_len) to attempt massive allocation, causing memory blowups/DoS. This is production-impacting because Diagnostic fields are public and spans are not validated at this boundary. Clamp/validate spans before arithmetic (e.g., saturating math + min/max against source bounds).
notes: Triage: debatable — unchecked usize subtraction exists but no current code path creates malformed spans. Defensive hardening only.
title: render_diagnostic can panic or OOM on invalid span ordering

close_reason: Implemented unit types across all compiler phases: lexer (UnitLiteral token), AST (UnitDecl + UnitLit), parser (unit decl + suffix literal), checker (Ty::UnitType, arithmetic/comparison rules), interpreter (eval + arithmetic ops), CLI (highlighter + types command)
description: Add unit types to the DSL as described in spec/v0/05_unit_types.ttrpg. A unit type wraps a single int field with type-safe arithmetic and optional literal suffix syntax (e.g., 30ft, 5gp). Declaration: unit TypeName suffix shortname { field: int }. Suffix is optional soft keyword. Lexer emits UnitLiteral tokens (digits+alpha, no space). Checker validates suffix maps to declared unit type. Arithmetic: same-type +/- returns unit, int*unit and unit*int scale, unit/unit returns float, unit/int and unit*unit are errors. Comparisons between same type produce bool, different types error. No implicit conversion to/from int.
title: Unit types: dimensioned numeric types with optional suffix literals

close_reason: Triaged: no measurable performance impact on realistic inputs. Small data sizes (3 outcomes, 5 options, 30 decls, 50 tokens) make algorithmic complexity irrelevant.
description: Ty::display constructs owned Strings for every call (including format! and clone()) and Display delegates through this allocation path. This is a code smell for a hot diagnostic/type-printing path. Refactor Display impl to write directly to fmt::Formatter (or expose a borrowed-display API) to reduce unnecessary allocations and cloning.
title: crates/ttrpg_checker/src/ty.rs: Avoid allocation-heavy Ty::display path

description: Resolver alias conflict check ignores enum variants (resolve.rs:175), and imported-name shadowing only compares against the alias target system, not all imported names (resolve.rs:193). close_reason: Resource bounds now resolved from AST field declarations at mutation time. eval_assign_entity looks up entity type → field def → navigates TypeExpr along path to find resource bounds → evaluates bound expressions (literals + entity field refs). MutateField effects now carry actual bounds, and adapter clamping works for both direct resource fields and resource-valued map entries. 8 integration tests verify clamping for underflow, overflow, set, auto-init, dynamic bounds, and optional groups.
description: Extend the mutation/adapter path so that writing to a map<K, resource(min..max)> entry applies resource clamping. When the adapter handles MutateField with path [Field(f), Index(k)], look up the field declaration for f. If value type is resource(...), extract bound expressions, evaluate them in entity context, and clamp the result. This is the core new work — bounds lookup must unwrap the map value type to find the inner resource bounds.
title: Adapter: clamp writes to resource-valued map entries

close_reason: Closed
description: eval_add, eval_sub, and eval_comparison guard unit branches with as_unit_value checks, then recompute as_unit_value and unwrap the results. Even when currently safe, this pattern is brittle and duplicates work on runtime paths. Capture unit tuples once with if let or match binding and avoid unwrap in evaluator logic.
title: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/eval.rs: Remove guard plus recompute plus unwrap pattern in unit ops

close_reason: Fixed: check for RParen before parsing bindings, allowing empty binding lists
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_parser/src/decl.rs

Why this is a bug: parse_trigger_param() always calls parse_trigger_bindings(), and parse_trigger_bindings() unconditionally parses at least one binding. This makes trigger clauses with empty binding lists unparseable. However, the AST/checker/runtime all support empty trigger binding vectors (matching by event name only), so parser behavior is inconsistent with the rest of the pipeline.

Potential impact: Reactions/hooks cannot target events without explicit bindings (including zero-parameter events), causing valid programs to fail at parse time.

Suggested fix: In parse_trigger_param(), mirror parse_modify_clause behavior: if the next token is RParen, use an empty vec; otherwise call parse_trigger_bindings().
title: Bug: Empty trigger binding list rejected

close_reason: Entity destruction is better modeled as conditions (Dead, Destroyed, Banished) rather than entity removal. TTRPGs almost universally keep entity data around for resurrection/repair. CLI 'destroy' command suffices for host-level cleanup.
description: No way to remove entities from the game. Death in 5e is permanent removal of a character, but the DSL has no destroy() or remove_entity() builtin. Entities are created via spawn but never destroyed. Needed for: character death, destroying objects, banishment effects.
title: Language: entity destruction / lifecycle management

close_reason: Used .unwrap() workaround to access fields on Option values in CLI scripts instead of implementing auto-unwrap
description: CLI scripts cannot access fields on Option-wrapped struct values (e.g. restore_result.restored_level when apply_restoration returns Option<RestoreResult>). This limits test assertions to assert_ne result, none rather than inspecting inner fields. Needed to fully migrate restoration field-level assertions from Rust to CLI.
title: CLI: support field access on Option values

close_reason: Closed
description: Exhaustively verify all type system rules from 01_type_system.ttrpg and 05_unit_types.ttrpg. Areas to cover: (1) Every row in the coercion table (DiceExpr→RollResult, RollResult→int, and all NEVER cases). (2) Every arithmetic combination: int±int, int/int→float, float±int, float±float, DiceExpr±DiceExpr, DiceExpr±int, DiceExpr*int rejection. (3) Unit type arithmetic: unit±unit(same), int*unit, unit/unit→float, and all error cases (unit+different_unit, unit+int, unit*unit, unit/int). (4) Unit type comparisons. (5) Resource type clamping on =, +=, -=. (6) Float: no literals, only produced by division, floor/ceil convert back. (7) Composite types: map/list/set/option construction and access patterns.
title: Spec audit: type system and arithmetic

description: While fixing tdsl-jtcy against OSRIC_3.0_Gamemaster_Guide.pdf, I verified that standard trolls, ice trolls, and similar variants do not necessarily take bonus fire/acid damage. Instead, their regeneration excludes fire and corrosive damage, which is what makes those damage types matter for permanent destruction. Current OSRIC DSL still represents this with VulnerableToFire/VulnerableToAcid markers and has no regeneration exception hook. Add a dedicated rule path for regeneration-excluded damage types and update affected bestiary entries accordingly.
title: Model troll-style regeneration exceptions for fire and acid

title: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/reference_state.rs: make read_enabled_options deterministic

close_reason: Implemented and verified
description: At level 1, osric/osric_chargen.ttrpg computes one averaged multiclass HP total and writes that same amount into every HpRoll entry for every class. osric/osric_level_drain.ttrpg later looks up HP by (class, level) and subtracts the recorded amount when one class is drained. That means draining one class can remove the character's full averaged level-1 HP instead of just that class's share. The hp_rolls history needs to record per-class averaged gains, not duplicate the total across classes. Reference: PG §1.3.11 multi-class hit points and §1.6.7 level drain. Affected files: osric/osric_chargen.ttrpg, osric/osric_level_drain.ttrpg.
title: Store per-class multiclass HP rolls for drain/restoration

close_reason: Fixed with regression tests enabled
description: crates/ttrpg_parser/src/lower.rs introduces a hard-coded local binding named result (line 227) and then reads that binding in generated guard arms (line 258). Because move receiver/params are user-controlled and not checked against this name during lowering, user code that uses a binding named result in outcome blocks gets captured by the synthetic binding. This can silently change behavior (for example comparisons now use roll result instead of the move argument) or produce confusing type errors. Lowering should generate a hygienic internal name or reject/conflict-check result in move bindings before synthesis.
title: Move lowering captures user result binding in synthesized action

close_reason: Not reachable: bind_args guarantees length matches fn_info.params, both from same source
description: collect_modifiers_owned indexes bound_params with bound_params[i] while iterating fn_info.params. If these slices ever diverge in length (runtime bug, host mismatch, future refactor), this will panic and abort interpretation. Replace with safe access (get(i)) and return a RuntimeError with context instead of panicking, so failures stay recoverable and diagnosable.
title: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/pipeline.rs: remove panic-prone bound_params indexing in collect_modifiers_owned

description: On unarmed to-hit success for grappling: roll 1d8 + modifiers on Table 1.6.12c. Results range from Awkward Scuffle (1 temp) through various holds (Arm Grab, Waist Lock, Rear Choke, Arm Lock, Head Lock) to Decisive Throw (9+). Each hold has controlling and inferior variants with different effects. Damage splits real/temp. Modifiers: attacker/defender appendages (1 arm: ±1, 3+: ±1), size (-4 Tiny to +10 Gargantuan attacker, inverse for defender). Ref: PG p110-111.
title: Implement grappling results table and modifiers (Table 1.6.12c)

close_reason: Completed
description: OSRIC dual-classing requires 15+ in the old class prime requisite(s) and 17+ in the new class prime requisite(s). osric/osric_dualclass.ttrpg treats Assassin and Illusionist as having no prime requisites, so dual-class eligibility becomes vacuously true for those classes. The Player Guide explicitly identifies Dexterity as the prime requisite for assassins and Intelligence as the prime requisite for illusionists. Reference: PG §1.3.12, §1.1.3, and §1.1.5. Affected file: osric/osric_dualclass.ttrpg.
title: Honor assassin and illusionist prime requisites in dual-class checks

close_reason: Completed spec audit of conditions/modify system. Fixed 2 checker bugs (diamond extends false-positive cycle detection, user-defined modify_applied override rejected). Added 21 integration tests covering all 7 areas: mixed Phase 1+2, conflict resolution, cost modification, suppress clauses, modify_applied override, extends inheritance/ordering/diamond, and parameterized condition removal.
description: Deep audit of the condition/modify system against 02_scoping.ttrpg. Runtime-focused tests needed. Areas: (1) Phase 1 (pre-call parameter rewrite) vs Phase 2 (post-call result rewrite) partitioning — verify a single modify body can contain both. (2) Conflict resolution: multiple conditions modifying same parameter, oldest-first pipeline ordering, last-writer-wins. (3) Cost modification (modify Action.cost): verify cost=free, cost=token replacement, rejection of param/result overrides in cost modify bodies. (4) Suppress clauses: verify reaction suppression at runtime, verify hooks are NOT suppressible. (5) modify_applied event: verify emission after modify fires, deduplication by condition instance id, user-defined override takes precedence. (6) Condition extends clause behavior. (7) Parameterized conditions: params stored on ActiveCondition, matching on remove.
title: Spec audit: conditions and modify system

In crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_stmt.rs, both check_grant and check_revoke state that these statements are only allowed in action or reaction context, but the gate uses scope.allows_mutation() (lines 290 and 389). close_reason: Closed
description:
The DSL has no loop construct. This blocks modeling:
- Extra Attack (make N attack rolls where N depends on level)
- AoE spells (apply damage to N targets)
- Multi-damage-type processing (iterate over list<DamageSpec>)
- Spell slot deduction (9-branch match instead of indexed access)

A bounded `for` loop would cover most cases:
    for target in targets { ... }
    for i in 1..attack_count(attacker) { ... }

Unbounded while loops are probably not needed for TTRPG modeling.
title: Language: bounded iteration / loops

close_reason: Already fixed in current codebase
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/state.rs

Why this is a bug: ActiveCondition::to_value stores id as Value::Int(self.id as i64). id is u64, so values above i64::MAX wrap/truncate on cast and become incorrect, even for valid host-provided IDs.

Potential impact: DSL-visible condition IDs can be corrupted, breaking comparisons, lookups, or diagnostics that rely on stable condition instance IDs.

Suggested fix: Preserve full u64 range by exposing id as a lossless representation (for example an unsigned value type or string), or guard and return an error on out-of-range values instead of lossy casting.
title: Bug: ActiveCondition id overflows when converted to Value::Int

close_reason: Resolved by design: explicit emit + mechanic wrappers (e.g. deal_damage) is the right pattern. Auto-emission from field mutations is too implicit and creates footguns (invisible cascades, infinite loops). Future privacy system will restrict direct field writes, making the mechanic-wrapper pattern compiler-enforced rather than convention-based.
notes: Partially addressed by emit statement (tdsl-wbic). DSL authors can now write `emit takes_damage(...)` alongside mutations. Remaining gap: mutations don't auto-emit — the emit must be written explicitly. Full auto-emission would require field-level on_decrease hooks.
title: Language: automatic event emission from mutations

close_reason: Map literal syntax {key: value} already existed; fixed empty map type inference via tail-hint propagation, added map_lit to spec grammar, and added 8 integration tests
description: The spec grammar has no map_lit production in primary_expr. Maps can be typed (map<K, V>) and indexed (m[key]), but there's no way to construct a map literal in DSL code. Maps can only be populated via CLI 'set' commands. This means derives/mechanics that need to return or construct maps have no way to do so. Possible syntaxes: [key: value, ...] (Python-like, conflicts with list literal + colon) or {key: value} (conflicts with struct literal / block). Needs design work.
title: No map literal syntax in DSL expressions

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/action.rs

Why this is a bug: In collect_and_apply_cost_modifiers(), binding expressions are evaluated with eval_expr, but Err(_) is converted into all_match = false rather than being returned. This suppresses runtime expression errors during cost-modifier binding checks.

Potential impact: Valid programs can silently skip applicable cost modifiers when a binding expression hits a runtime error (for example division by zero), producing incorrect behavior and making failures hard to diagnose.

Suggested fix: Propagate eval_expr errors from binding evaluation (return Err) instead of treating them as a non-match, aligning behavior with modify binding checks in pipeline.rs.
title: Bug: Cost modify binding eval errors are silently ignored

close_reason: Already fixed in bd4d2d1 — uses (expr.modifier as i128).unsigned_abs() with regression test
description: In crates/ttrpg_cli/src/format.rs lines 127-129, negative modifiers are rendered with expr.modifier.abs(). DiceExpr.modifier is i64, so i64::MIN is a valid runtime value. abs() on i64::MIN overflows: with overflow checks enabled this can panic; without checks it stays negative and produces malformed output like 1d20 - -9223372036854775808. This is user-visible CLI corruption and can crash debug builds. Suggested fix: use checked_abs()/unsigned_abs() and special-case i64::MIN so formatting never overflows.
title: format_dice_expr overflows/misformats i64::MIN modifier

close_reason: Closed
description:
Allow 'as <alias>' syntax at the two points where optional group existence is proven:

1. **`with` constraints**: `action CastSpell on caster: Character with AdvancedSpellcasting as sc (cost: int)` — alias scoped to the function body
2. **`has` guards**: `if caster has AdvancedSpellcasting as sc { caster.sc.spell_slots -= cost }` — alias scoped to the guard block

This lets users avoid repeating long group names in field access paths. The alias is always locally scoped (no global renaming), and only valid where narrowing has been proven.

Touches: parser (parse 'as ident' after group name in with/has), AST (store optional alias on WithConstraint and HasExpr), checker (register alias in scope for field resolution), interpreter (resolve alias during field access).
title: Add alias bindings for optional groups at narrowing sites

close_reason: Design choice: inherent brace ambiguity (map literal vs block) — same as Rust/Go/etc. Parenthesized workaround exists
description: Bug in crates/ttrpg_parser/src/expr.rs at parse_arm_body (lines 654-659). Arm bodies branch to parse_block whenever the next token is LBrace. Since map literals are expression forms starting with LBrace (parse_primary_expr lines 313-340), an arm like `k => {"x": 1}` is forced down block parsing and fails instead of parsing as ArmBody::Expr(MapLit). Repro: ttrpg_parser::parse_expr("match n { 1 => {\"x\": 1}, _ => {\"x\": 0} }") should parse, but currently errors unless each map literal is parenthesized.
title: match arm body cannot be map literal without extra parentheses

description: File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/eval.rs (collect_idents lines 2257-2291, eval_bound_expr lines 2299-2331, resolve_resource_bounds lines 2338-2359). eval_bound_expr retries bound evaluation by collecting identifiers and binding entity fields, but collect_idents does not traverse many expression variants (notably If then/else bodies, IfLet, PatternMatch, GuardMatch, StructLit, etc.). If a bound expression references entity fields inside one of those omitted subtrees, fallback misses required bindings, evaluation fails, and resolve_resource_bounds returns None. That silently drops bounds for MutateField, so clamping is skipped and out-of-range mutations can pass in production.
notes: Triage: real but low practical risk — collect_idents misses some expression forms but resource bounds are almost always simple expressions like 0..=max_HP.
title: resource bound fallback misses identifiers in many expression forms

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_cli/src/runner/parse.rs

Why this is a bug: parse_spawn_block rejects duplicate field keys but does not reject duplicate inline group names. The returned groups vector can contain the same group multiple times, and cmd_spawn later writes each prepared group in order. This means a single spawn command can declare the same group twice and the last one silently wins, which is inconsistent with duplicate field handling and makes input order change semantics unexpectedly.

Potential impact: Users can accidentally provide conflicting values for the same optional group in one spawn block and get silently overwritten state instead of a validation error, leading to hard-to-debug incorrect character/entity setup.

Suggested fix: Track seen group names in parse_spawn_block (or before applying prepared groups) and return an explicit duplicate-group error when a name repeats, matching duplicate field behavior.
title: Bug: spawn allows duplicate inline groups with silent overwrite

close_reason: Fixed with regression tests enabled
description: In crates/ttrpg_checker/src/collect.rs:1072-1081, collect_option immediately validates o.extends by checking env.options.contains(parent_name). Because options are inserted incrementally during pass_1b, this rejects valid forward references when a child option appears before its parent declaration. The checker then emits a false option extends unknown option error. This conflicts with the later whole-program validation in crates/ttrpg_checker/src/collect.rs:1085-1140 (validate_option_extends), which already runs after all declarations are collected. Impact: valid programs become order-dependent and fail compilation.
title: Option extends validation is order-dependent in collect pass

close_reason: Closed
description: Conditions are purely declarative today (modify + suppress). They cannot execute code when applied, removed, or each round. This blocks: bleeding at 0 HP (1 HP/round loss), poison over time, ongoing spell effects (Wall of Fire damage each round), condition side-effects (Unconscious should also apply Prone/drop items). Current workaround (orphan hooks on RoundStart that check for conditions) separates behavior from definition and only works inside resolve_round(). Proposed syntax: on_apply/on_tick/on_remove blocks inside condition declarations. Needs design doc covering: syntax, scoping rules (can on_tick mutate? emit?), tick trigger mechanism (who calls tick — interpreter or DSL event?), interaction with duration/expiry.
notes: Decision (2026-03-05): on_tick was considered and rejected for Phase 2. Periodic condition effects (bleeding, poison, ongoing spell damage) use explicit functions called from the combat loop instead. Rationale: "tick" is ambiguous across time scales; explicit functions preserve visible ordering; no new interpreter machinery needed. Auto-expiry is already handled by expire_conditions(). Condition state deferred until a concrete use case emerges.
title: Design: condition lifecycle hooks (on_apply/on_remove) — Phase 1

close_reason: Character entity restructured with HitPoints, EquipmentSlots include groups and Spellcasting optional group. All tests passing.
description:
Restructure OSRIC Character entity to use restricted groups for field encapsulation.

## Plan

### 1. EquipmentSlots group (OSRIC Equipment)
- Move wielded_main, wielded_off, worn_armor, worn_shield, current_weight into an included group EquipmentSlots declared in OSRIC Equipment
- Mark all fields restricted
- Invariant: current_weight must stay in sync with equipped items via equip/unequip functions
- Access: included, so reads like char.wielded_main still work

### 2. HitPoints group (OSRIC Combat)
- Move hp and max_hp into an included group HitPoints declared in OSRIC Combat
- Mark hp restricted (max_hp stays unrestricted)
- Invariant: damage must flow through TakeDamage for events/reduction/death checks
- Access: included, so reads like char.hp still work

### 3. Spellcasting group (OSRIC Spells)
- Move Spellcasting optional group declaration from OSRIC core to OSRIC Spells
- Mark memorised_spells, slots_used, casting_invocation restricted
- Move BeginCasting action from OSRIC Initiative to OSRIC Spells (casting logic belongs with spells)
- Invariant: slots_used syncs with memorised_spells; casting_invocation tracks active state
- spell_slots stays unrestricted (set during character creation)

## Implementation order
1. EquipmentSlots group
2. HitPoints group
3. Spellcasting group + move BeginCasting
title: Migrate OSRIC code: refactor Character into groups for restricted fields

Only the opening { suppresses the next NL, not all NLs inside braces. Workaround: put everything on one line, or wrap in parens. See pain point #18 in examples/dnd5e_expanded.ttrpg.

close_reason: Design choice: inherent brace ambiguity (guard match vs map scrutinee) — parenthesized workaround exists
description: Bug in crates/ttrpg_parser/src/expr.rs at parse_match_expr (lines 507-514). The parser chooses guard match solely when the token after match is LBrace. Because map literals are parsed from LBrace in parse_primary_expr (lines 313-340), a valid pattern-match scrutinee that starts with a map literal is misparsed. Repro: ttrpg_parser::parse_expr("match {\"k\": 1} { _ => 0 }") should parse as PatternMatch with a MapLit scrutinee, but is routed into parse_guard_match and then errors. This blocks brace-leading expressions as match scrutinees unless users add extra parentheses.
title: match misclassifies map-literal scrutinee as guard match

close_reason: Fixed in commit 313628b (Fix 4 REPL/CLI bugs)
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_cli/src/completer.rs

Why this is a bug: In the do, call, enable, and disable branches, the current token is derived with rest.trim(), but replacement start is computed as pos - current.len(). When the cursor is after a trailing space (for example do Attack ), trim() drops that space and word_start shifts right by one (or more), so the replacement span no longer matches the actual token. Accepting a completion can replace the wrong substring and corrupt input (for example producing do AAttack).

Potential impact: Tab completion can modify valid user input into invalid commands, causing confusing behavior and failed command execution in common interactive workflows.

Suggested fix: Derive the active token without trimming trailing whitespace (for example use a last-token parser like last_word(rest)), or detect trailing whitespace and avoid suggesting/replacing the previous token; compute span start from the true token boundary in line_to_cursor.
title: Bug: Completion span misaligned after trailing space

close_reason: Implemented ordered enums with ordinal/from_ordinal builtins
description:
Enums have no inherent ordering. In 5e, Size (tiny < small < medium < large < huge < gargantuan) needs comparison:
    requires { target.size <= grappler.size + 1 }
This is impossible because Size is an enum without ordinal values. Workaround is != checks for specific variants, which doesn't generalize.

Options:
- Ordered enums: `enum Size ordered { tiny, small, medium, large, huge, gargantuan }`
- Enum-to-int builtin: `ordinal(Size.medium) -> 2`
- Comparable enums that support <, >, <=, >= based on declaration order
title: Language: ordered enums / enum-to-int conversion

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_parser/src/types.rs

Why this is a bug: In parse_type, the "option" branch only handles option<T> and otherwise returns TypeExpr::Named("option") immediately. Unlike the generic fallback path, it never checks for .Ident to form TypeExpr::Qualified. This means valid qualified types such as option.CustomType (when use "Other" as option is present) are misparsed, leaving .CustomType unconsumed and causing downstream parse errors.

Potential impact: Programs that use option as a module alias cannot reference imported types via qualified syntax, producing parse failures in otherwise valid code.

Suggested fix: In the "option" case, keep option<T> handling, but for non-< cases fall through to the same named/qualified logic used by the default identifier branch (including .Ident handling) instead of returning Named("option") directly.
title: Bug: parse_type rejects qualified types with option alias

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/call/actions.rs

Why this is a bug: In dispatch_action_method, bind_args is called with only fn_info.params and action_decl.params, excluding the receiver from the binding context. bind_args evaluates default expressions in a temporary scope containing already-bound parameters. For action defaults that reference the receiver (allowed by checker because receiver is in scope when defaults are type-checked), method-call syntax (entity.Action()) evaluates those defaults without receiver bound and raises an undefined-variable runtime error on valid programs.

Potential impact: Valid DSL programs fail at runtime when calling actions with omitted optional args whose defaults reference the receiver, but the equivalent function-style action call works. This is inconsistent behavior between call syntaxes and causes unexpected runtime failures.

Suggested fix: In dispatch_action_method, evaluate defaults in an effective parameter context that includes the receiver as a first bound slot (mirroring dispatch_action), or explicitly push a scope and bind the receiver name/value before calling bind_args so default expressions can resolve it.
title: Bug: Action method defaults cannot reference receiver

close_reason: Implemented and verified
description: The current Spellcasting model stores one spell_slots list and one memorised_spells list. Chargen picks only the first caster class in a multiclass build, and level-up overwrites Spellcasting.spell_slots using the class that just advanced. That cannot represent legal OSRIC cases such as cleric/magic-user and cleric/fighter/magic-user, and it also drops the ranger's separate magic-user spell progression even though ranger_mu_slots() exists. Reference: PG §1.3.9 ranger spellcasting, §1.3.11 multiclassing, and class spell tables in Chapter 3. Affected files: osric/osric_spells.ttrpg, osric/osric_chargen.ttrpg, osric/osric_levelup.ttrpg, osric/osric_core.ttrpg.
title: Support multi-tradition spell slots for rangers and multi-class casters

close_reason: Implemented in checker and interpreter with 8 tests
description:
Support method-call syntax for action invocation. Currently actions are called as functions with the receiver as the first argument: Attack(attacker, target, weapon). This adds a method-call form: attacker.Attack(target, weapon), which desugars to the function-call form.

## Motivation
- Natural readability: 'attacker.Attack(target, weapon)' reads as subject-verb-object
- Consistency with existing method syntax on collections, options, dice, strings
- Actions already have an explicit receiver via 'on receiver: Type' — method syntax is the natural complement
- PascalCase action names visually distinguish from lowercase builtin methods

## Design (spec updated in 03_canonical_grammar.ttrpg)
- Both forms are equivalent: method form desugars to function form
- Resolution order for obj.Name(args): enum constructor > module alias > action dispatch > builtin method > error
- Action methods checked before builtin methods (PascalCase vs lowercase avoids collision)
- Context restrictions still apply (action/reaction/hook resolve blocks only)
- Named arguments work in both forms

## Implementation plan

### Checker (check_expr.rs)
1. In check_method_call(), before the catch-all error arm, add an action lookup:
   - Use env.lookup_fn(method) to find a FnInfo with kind == FnKind::Action
   - Verify the action's receiver type is compatible with obj_ty
   - Check context restrictions (must be in action/reaction/hook resolve block)
   - Build effective_params WITHOUT receiver (obj already fills that role)
   - Type-check remaining args against action params
   - Return the action's return type (unit)
2. Alternatively, intercept in the FieldAccess branch of check_call() before calling check_method_call()

### Interpreter (call.rs)
1. In eval_method_call(), before the catch-all error arm, add action dispatch:
   - Check if method name is an action in the program
   - The object value must be Value::Entity
   - Construct the full argument list with object as receiver, then remaining args
   - Call dispatch_action() with the synthetic argument list
2. Alternatively, intercept in the FieldAccess branch of eval_call() before calling eval_method_call()

### Tests
- Basic method syntax: entity.Action(args) works
- Equivalence: both forms produce the same result
- Named args: entity.Action(target: x, weapon: y)
- Error: method syntax on non-entity type
- Error: action name not matching receiver type
- Error: calling in wrong context (derive, etc.)
- No collision with builtin methods (different naming conventions)
title: Method-call syntax for actions: entity.Action(args)

close_reason: Fixed all 5 interpreter/checker name resolution divergences
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/eval/compare.rs

Why this is a bug: match_pattern() always treats PatternKind::Ident(name) as an enum-variant pattern when name exists in type_env.variant_to_enums. The checker intentionally allows bare identifiers to be variable bindings in binding/destructure contexts (check_pattern_binding and check_pattern_inner(..., in_destructure=true, ...)), even when the identifier matches a known variant name. Runtime and checker semantics therefore diverge for valid programs.

Potential impact: Valid for/comprehension patterns and destructuring subpatterns can fail to match or fail to bind variables when the binding name collides with any enum variant name (for example: for red in xs, or Variant(red) where red should bind a field). This causes incorrect control-flow and missed matches at runtime.

Suggested fix: Carry pattern context into runtime matching (binding-context / in-destructure flags) or use checker-resolved variant metadata so only syntactic variant patterns are matched as variants. In binding contexts, PatternKind::Ident should always bind.
title: Bug: match_pattern treats binding idents as enum variants

close_reason: Duplicate of tdsl-ljhg. Same unchecked sum() bug in two code paths (methods.rs and collection_builtins.rs). Keeping tdsl-ljhg to track both.
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/call/collection_builtins.rs

Why this is a bug: eval_sum accumulates integer lists with int_sum += n instead of checked arithmetic. On large valid int inputs (for example [i64::MAX, 1]), this can panic in debug builds or wrap in release builds rather than returning a RuntimeError, unlike other arithmetic paths that use checked_add.

Potential impact: Valid programs can crash interpreter executions or produce silently incorrect wrapped totals when summing large integer lists.

Suggested fix: Replace unchecked integer accumulation with checked_add and return RuntimeError::with_span on overflow, matching existing arithmetic overflow behavior.
title: Bug: sum() can overflow int accumulation

That helper is used by spawn field validation (lines 500-517), set validation (lines 730-739), and grant group-field validation (lines 1082-1100). This allows assigning an entity of the wrong concrete type into fields declared as a specific entity type, violating checker compatibility rules and allowing invalid typed references into runtime state.

description: When using 'check -c', the next argument is taken as the literal source string. There's no way to pipe source into check via stdin. Attempting 'cat file | ttrpg check -c -' treats '-' as the literal source text (a minus sign), not as a stdin indicator. This makes it awkward to use check in shell pipelines or with heredocs. The run command supports pipe mode (non-terminal stdin), but check -c has no equivalent. Either: (1) make 'check -c -' read from stdin, (2) make 'check' without arguments read from stdin when it's a pipe (like run does), or (3) both.
