OSRIC 3.0 sections 1.6.2.1 and 1.6.2.10 require: defender with longer weapon attacks first, charger loses DEX AC bonus, charge frequency limit (once per 10 rounds), and set weapon strike in segment 1 for double damage. Implement full charge/set interaction semantics.
design:
# Charge / Set-vs-Charge + Round Tracking Design

## Overview

Full implementation of OSRIC 3.0 §1.6.2.1 (Charging Into Combat) and §1.6.2.10 (Set Weapon Against Charge), plus the general-purpose round/time tracking infrastructure needed to support it (and spell durations, memorization, etc.).

## Game Time Model

### Unified counter (segments as base unit)
A single monotonically increasing int representing game time in segments:
- 1 round = 10 segments
- 1 turn = 100 segments (10 rounds)
- 1 hour = 600 segments
- 1 day = 14,400 segments

Comparison and arithmetic are trivial int ops. Structured accessors (to_round, to_turn, etc.) provide readability.

### Surprise is off-clock
Surprise segments happen before the game clock starts. The surprise resolution code manages segment-scoped effects locally (knows exactly how many surprise segments there are). The game clock begins at round 1, segment 1 (counter value 0 or 1).

Effects that need to carry from surprise into combat get promoted to round-based durations by the surprise code.

### Duration enum (expanded)
```
enum Duration {
    end_of_round,
    segments(count: int),   // managed locally (surprise, casting), not via game clock
    rounds(count: int),
    turns(count: int),
    hours(count: int),
    days(count: int),
    indefinite
}
```

`segments(n)` exists as a variant but is handled by local code (surprise resolution, casting time tracking) rather than the game clock expiry system. All other timed variants map to segment counts for clock-based expiry.

### Duration to segments mapping
```
derive duration_to_segments(d: Duration) -> option<int> {
    match d {
        Duration.end_of_round => some(10),
        Duration.segments(n)  => some(n),       // only used by local code
        Duration.rounds(n)    => some(n * 10),
        Duration.turns(n)     => some(n * 100),
        Duration.hours(n)     => some(n * 600),
        Duration.days(n)      => some(n * 14400),
        Duration.indefinite   => none
    }
}
```

## Condition Expiry (DSL-side)

### Interpreter changes
- Add `applied_at: i64` field to `ActiveCondition` (Rust struct)
- Add `env.current_game_time: Option<i64>` to interpreter Env
- `apply_condition` stamps `applied_at` from `env.current_game_time`
- `to_value()` exposes `applied_at` as an int field to DSL code
- Host sets `env.current_game_time` before calling DSL functions

### DSL-side expiry function
```
function expire_conditions(game_time: int, combatants: list<Character>) {
    for c in combatants {
        for cond in conditions(c) {
            let limit = duration_to_segments(cond.duration)
            if limit.is_some() {
                let elapsed = game_time - cond.applied_at
                if elapsed >= limit.unwrap() {
                    remove_condition(c, cond)
                }
            }
        }
    }
}
```

### Round resolution integration
```
function resolve_round(
    game_time: int,          // NEW: current game time in segments
    round_number: int,       // NEW: for fighter attack alternation, cooldowns
    initiative: InitiativeResult,
    actions: list<RoundAction>
) {
    emit RoundStart(round: round_number, game_time: game_time)
    // ... existing segment loop (for seg in 1..=10) ...
    emit RoundEnd(round: round_number, game_time: game_time)
    // Expire timed conditions at round end
    // (combatant list derived from actions)
}
```

## Charge Rules (OSRIC 3.0 §1.6.2.1)

### What's needed
1. Weapon length data: add `length` field to MeleeWeaponDef (from Table 1.4.2.3c)
2. Charging condition: strips DEX AC bonus, -1 AC penalty if no DEX bonus
3. Charge cooldown: once per 10 rounds, tracked via `last_charge_time` entity field
4. Longer weapon first strike: if defender has longer weapon, attacks before charger
5. Encumbrance restriction: max encumbrance cannot charge unless mounted
6. Mounted lance double: lance + warhorse doubles weapon damage (not STR/magic/spec)

### Charge-vs-Set interaction (resolved in ChargeAction branch of resolve_action)
When ChargeAction resolves:
1. Apply Charging condition (loses DEX AC)
2. Check if target declared SetAgainstCharge in same round
3. If set: defender strikes first with 2x damage
4. If no set but defender has longer weapon: defender strikes first (normal damage)
5. If charger survives: resolve charge attack with +2 to-hit
6. Record last_charge_time for cooldown tracking

### Set-vs-Charge (§1.6.2.10)
- Set weapon readied at segment 1 (already implemented)
- Cannot attack unless actually charged
- Valid weapons: spear, javelin, lance (dismounted), pole arm, trident (already implemented)
- Double damage on hit against charger
title: Charge and set-vs-charge rules are only partially implemented

close_reason: Fixed: check for RParen before parsing bindings, allowing empty binding lists
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_parser/src/decl.rs

Why this is a bug: parse_suppress_clause() always calls parse_modify_bindings(), and parse_modify_bindings() unconditionally parses at least one binding. This makes suppress clauses with empty binding lists unparseable. Downstream code handles empty suppress bindings (interpreted as unconditional match for the event), so parser behavior is inconsistent with checker/runtime semantics.

Potential impact: Conditions cannot express unconditional suppression of an event, and otherwise valid suppress clauses fail during parsing.

Suggested fix: In parse_suppress_clause(), allow empty parentheses by checking for RParen before calling parse_modify_bindings().
title: Bug: Empty suppress binding list rejected

close_reason: Fixed in commit 313628b (Fix 4 REPL/CLI bugs)
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_cli/src/runner/inspect.rs

Why this is a bug: In cmd_inspect, the three-segment path branch (handle.Group.field) reads state first and, when the Group key is absent, falls back to reporting '<handle>.<Group> is not currently granted' unless the group is declared as required. For an undeclared group name (e.g. inspect hero.Foo.bar where Foo is not a group/field on the entity type), this produces an incorrect diagnostic instead of an unknown group/field error.

Potential impact: Users receive misleading errors for typos and invalid inspect paths, which obscures actual schema problems and makes debugging harder.

Suggested fix: In the handle.Group.field branch, validate group_name against the entity type schema first (lookup_optional_group) and return an unknown-group error when absent; only use granted/missing status messaging for declared groups.
title: Bug: inspect misreports unknown Group.field as not granted

close_reason: Fixed all 5 interpreter/checker name resolution divergences
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/eval/dispatch.rs

Why this is a bug: eval_ident resolves identifiers in a different order and visibility scope than checker check_ident. Runtime checks enum types and module aliases before conditions, and module aliases are matched across all systems (system_aliases.values().any(...)) instead of current-system visibility. A program that type-checks (for example, a condition name that collides with an enum type or an alias in another system) can evaluate to a different runtime value (EnumNamespace/ModuleAlias instead of Condition).

Potential impact: Well-typed programs can execute incorrect branches, pass wrong values to condition APIs, or fail at runtime due to mis-bound identifiers. Behavior can change based on unrelated declarations in other namespaces/systems.

Suggested fix: Make runtime identifier resolution mirror checker semantics exactly: same precedence order as check_ident and visibility filtering by current system/module metadata (not global alias scan).
title: Bug: eval_ident name resolution diverges from checker

close_reason: Implemented condition declaration tags: AST, parser, checker validation, runtime exposure as Set<string> on ActiveCondition.tags, test script, and documentation updates
description:
Make tags from condition declarations (e.g. #curse, #disease, #poison) accessible at runtime on ActiveCondition values. Expose as c.tags field (Set<String>) so DSL code can categorically filter conditions by their declaration tags.

IMPORTANT DESIGN DISTINCTION:
- Condition-level tags are for INHERENTLY STATIC properties of the condition type — things that are always true regardless of how the condition was applied (e.g. #curse, #disease, #poison). Bestow Curse is always a curse; Mummy Rot is always a disease.
- Source-dependent properties like 'magical' belong on EffectSource, NOT condition tags, because the same condition (e.g. Paralyzed) can come from magical sources (Hold Person spell) or non-magical sources (ghoul venom). Magicalness is a property of the application, not the condition type.
- Modify-clause tags describe individual mechanical effects (what the effect DOES: #bonus, #penalty) and are used for targeted suppression within a condition.

Steps:
1. Add tags: Vec<Name> to ConditionDecl in AST
2. Parse #tag annotations on condition declarations
3. Carry tags through checker (validate against declared tag namespace)
4. Store tags on ActiveCondition at runtime
5. Expose as Set<String> via ActiveCondition.to_value()
6. Document condition-level tags in spec, language reference, and authoring guide — clarify distinction from modify-clause tags and from EffectSource
title: Expose condition declaration tags at runtime

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_parser/src/resolve.rs

Why this is a bug: desugar_qualified_types claims to walk all type expressions, but desugar_decl_types only calls desugar_modify_stmts for modify clauses and never visits ModifyClause.target. Selector predicates in ModifyTarget::Selector can contain TypeExpr via SelectorPredicate::Returns and SelectorPredicate::HasParam { ty }. Qualified types in those positions remain as TypeExpr::Qualified, so checker code later reports qualified type requires module resolution instead of resolving valid aliases.

Potential impact: Valid programs using qualified types in modify selectors fail type checking with false errors; alias and type ownership validation is skipped for these type positions.

Suggested fix: Extend resolver traversal to desugar selector predicate types in ModifyClause.target (for both condition and option modify clauses), recursing through Returns and optional HasParam type expressions before checking.
title: Bug: Qualified types in modify selectors are not resolved

close_reason: all steps complete
description:
Add support for tagging modify clauses within conditions, enabling two capabilities:

1. **Targeted suppression by category**: Conditions can suppress entire categories of modifiers rather than needing to know specific condition names. E.g., `suppress [#position](target: bearer)` removes all position-based penalties, `suppress [#fear](target: bearer)` removes all fear effects.

2. **Audit trail / provenance**: The REPL can show a breakdown of where numeric modifiers come from, grouped by tag. E.g., `breakdown attack_roll(target: Thog)` shows each modifier with its tag, source condition, and value.

**Syntax sketch:**
```
condition Prone on bearer: entity {
    modify [#attack_resolution](target: bearer) #position #penalty {
        attack_mod = attack_mod + 4
    }
}

condition FreedomOfMovement on bearer: entity {
    suppress [#position](target: bearer)
}
```

**Scope:**
- Parser: allow tags after modify clause bindings, before body
- AST: store tags on ModifyClause
- Checker: validate tags exist
- Interpreter: filter tagged modify clauses during suppress evaluation
- REPL: add breakdown command showing provenance by tag

**Not in scope:** Removing boolean params from resolve_melee_attack (that is better solved by decomposing mechanics into sub-functions). Also not in scope: bonus type stacking rules (keep the DSL system-agnostic).
design:
## Design: Tags on Modify Clauses

### Syntax

Tags on modify clauses — after bindings, before body (consistent with action/function tag placement):

```
condition Prone on bearer: entity {
    modify [#attack_resolution](target: bearer) #position #penalty {
        attack_mod = attack_mod + 4
    }
}
```

### Suppress-Modify via Selector

New suppress variant using `[selector]` syntax (disambiguated from event suppress by leading `[`):

```
condition FreedomOfMovement on bearer: entity {
    suppress [#position](target: bearer)
}
```

- Supports full selector predicates: `#tag`, `returns Type`, `has param`
- Bindings are optional — omitting them suppresses all matching modify clauses regardless of entity
- Self-suppression is allowed (universal application, user error if contradictory)

### Semantics

**Suppress-modify evaluation** (in collect_modifiers_owned):
1. Collect all active suppress-modify clauses from entities in scope
2. For each candidate modifier, check if any suppress-modify clause matches by (a) tag/selector overlap and (b) binding match
3. Filter out suppressed modifiers before running phase 1/phase 2

**Binding match**: `suppress [#position](target: bearer)` means suppress any modify clause tagged #position where a binding named `target` resolves to this condition's bearer.

**Ordering**: Gained-order applies. Later-gained suppress overrides earlier-gained modifiers. "Un-suppress" (suppress a suppress) is out of scope for v0.

### Provenance

- `Effect::ModifyApplied` extended with modify clause tags
- REPL `breakdown` command shows per-modifier provenance: condition → tags → param/result changes → values
- Suppressed modifiers shown annotated (not omitted) for debugging

### AST Changes

- `ModifyClause`: add `tags: Vec<Name>`
- New `ConditionClause::SuppressModify` variant (separate from event suppress for clarity)

### Cost-modify tags deferred

Tags on `modify X.cost(...)` clauses are out of scope — separate concern with own binding semantics.

### Implementation Phases

1. AST: Add `tags: Vec<Name>` to `ModifyClause`
2. Parser: Parse `#tag` after modify bindings, before `{`
3. Checker: Validate modify clause tags exist
4. Interpreter: Include tags in `Effect::ModifyApplied`
5. AST: New `SuppressModify` variant
6. Parser: Parse `suppress [selector](bindings)`
7. Checker: Validate suppress-modify clauses
8. Interpreter: Filter suppressed modifiers in `collect_modifiers_owned`
9. REPL: `breakdown` command
10. Docs: Update spec and documentation
title: Add tags on modify clauses for provenance and targeted suppression

description: During the conditions/modify spec audit (tdsl-c92x), we found that ActiveCondition is only available as an internal Ty::ActiveCondition in the checker — users cannot reference it by name in DSL source code (e.g., as a parameter type in a user-defined event). The spec grammar (03_canonical_grammar.ttrpg) lists ActiveCondition as a valid type keyword, and the builtin modify_applied event uses it for its condition parameter, but the parser/checker don't resolve the name 'ActiveCondition' from user code. This blocks user-defined override of modify_applied with the correct parameter type. Fix: add ActiveCondition to the type resolution path (either as a TypeExpr variant or a Named type that resolves to Ty::ActiveCondition).
title: Expose ActiveCondition as a nameable type in DSL source

close_reason: Closed
description:
In crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_stmt.rs, the direct assignment branch in check_assign (lines 96-108) only checks mutable binding and does not forbid assigning to the implicit turn binding. Because turn is mutable, turn = ... type-checks. This conflicts with runtime behavior, which rejects direct turn reassignment and only permits field mutation (for example turn.actions -= 1). The mismatch causes a late runtime error instead of a checker error.

File references:
- crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_stmt.rs:96-108
- crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_stmt.rs:147-149
title: Checker allows direct turn reassignment that runtime rejects

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/eval/helpers.rs

Why this is a bug: resolve_resource_bounds only passes a one-segment prefix for group fields (or no prefix for normal fields) into eval_bound_expr. When a resource type is nested in a named struct (for example stats.hp: resource(0..=max_hp)), identifier lookup in the bound expression resolves max_hp as a top-level entity field instead of stats.max_hp (or Group.stats.max_hp). That causes bound evaluation to return None and disables clamping for valid nested resource declarations.

Potential impact: Assignments to nested resource fields can bypass min/max enforcement and write invalid values (for example negative HP) whenever bounds reference sibling fields in the same nested struct.

Suggested fix: Track and pass the full container path prefix to eval_bound_expr (all consumed FieldPathSegment::Field segments leading to the resource container), not just the optional-group root segment. Use that full prefix when resolving identifier names from resource bound expressions.
title: Bug: Nested struct resource bounds use wrong lookup prefix

close_reason: Implemented multi-class data model: ClassLevel struct, ClassingMode enum, helper/aggregation derives, all tests passing
description: Character currently has class: Class (single) and level: int (single). Multi-classing needs 2-3 simultaneous classes, dual-classing needs old+new class with level tracking. Proposed: change to classes: map<Class, int> mapping class to level. All derives that use class/level (bthb, saving_throws_for, xp_for_level, fighter_attacks_per_round, spell slots, etc.) must iterate over all held classes and pick best or aggregate. Consider: primary_class field for XP allocation, active_class for dual-classing restrictions, HP calculation (roll per class, divide by num classes). This touches osric_core, osric_class, osric_combat, osric_saves, osric_character, osric_initiative.
design:
## Data Model

### New types (osric_core.ttrpg)

```
struct ClassLevel {
    class: Class
    level: int
    xp: int
}

enum ClassingMode { Single, Multi, Dual }
```

### Character entity changes

Remove:
- `class: Class`
- `level: int = 1`
- `xp: int = 0`

Add:
- `classes: list<ClassLevel>`
- `classing_mode: ClassingMode`

### Accessor conventions

- `first(char.classes).unwrap()` — primary/only class (Single), or first class (Multi/Dual original)
- `last(char.classes).unwrap()` — current class for Dual, same as first for Single
- `max([...])` / iteration — best-of aggregation for BTHB, saves

### Rule divergence by mode

| Mechanic | Single | Multi | Dual |
|---|---|---|---|
| HP | class HD | averaged across classes | summed |
| BTHB | class table | best across classes | best across classes |
| Saves | class table | best per category | best per category |
| Armour | class permission | most restrictive | current class only (while dormant) |
| XP gain | all to class | split evenly | all to current class |
| Abilities | all active | all active | old dormant until new level > old level |

### Dual-class dormancy

Derived, not stored: `classes[1].level <= classes[0].level` means original class is dormant. No extra field needed.

### Files requiring changes

1. **osric_core** — entity + new types
2. **osric_combat** — `effective_target_ac` Monk checks, `resolve_melee/missile_attack` BTHB + Monk damage (use best_bthb, check any class == Monk)
3. **osric_saves** — `saving_throws_for` → aggregate best per category
4. **osric_character** — `prime_req_xp_bonus` → per-class or primary class
5. **osric_class** — `check_level_up` → per ClassLevel entry
6. **osric_initiative** — `has_fighter_multi_attack` → check any fighter-group class

### New aggregation derives needed

- `best_bthb(char) -> int` — max BTHB across all classes
- `best_saves(char) -> SavingThrows` — best per save category
- `effective_armour_permission(char) -> ArmourPermission` — mode-dependent
- `is_class(char, class) -> bool` — true if any ClassLevel matches (replaces `char.class == X`)
- `class_level(char, class) -> option<int>` — level for a specific class if held
- `has_active_class(char, class) -> bool` — like is_class but respects dual-class dormancy
- `primary_class(char) -> ClassLevel` — first entry (original/only)
- `current_class(char) -> ClassLevel` — last entry (active for dual, same as primary for single/multi)
title: Design: multi-class data model for Character entity

close_reason: Fixed all 4 alias/shadow resolution bugs
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_checker/src/env.rs

Why this is a bug: resolve_cost_token applies legacy aliases (e.g., action -> actions) before checking exact field-name matches. If a ruleset defines both singular and plural fields (for example, action and actions), cost token 'action' resolves to the alias target 'actions', making the exact 'action' field unreachable in cost clauses.

Potential impact: Valid schemas with both singular/plural resource names can have costs bound to the wrong field, causing incorrect type checking and runtime behavior.

Suggested fix: Prefer exact field-name matches before applying legacy alias remapping, or only apply aliases when no exact field exists.
title: Bug: Cost token alias resolution can shadow exact field names

close_reason: Fixed all 5 interpreter/checker name resolution divergences
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/eval/access.rs

Why this is a bug: In Value::ModuleAlias field access, variant resolution checks resolved_variants by expr.span and then falls back to unique_variant_owner(field). The checker alias path (resolve_alias_field) determines the target enum within the aliased system, but does not populate resolved_variants for this span. For valid code like Alias.Variant where the variant is unique in the aliased system but duplicated in another system, runtime lookup falls through and raises an error even though type checking succeeded.

Potential impact: Valid module-alias expressions can fail at runtime with no type, variant, or condition ... in module alias, breaking cross-system enum access when variant names overlap across systems.

Suggested fix: Persist alias-field variant resolution from the checker (for example insert into resolved_variants for the field-access span), or in runtime ModuleAlias handling resolve variants using alias target metadata instead of global unique_variant_owner.
title: Bug: Alias-qualified enum variant lookup ignores checker resolution

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/call/methods.rs

Why this is a bug: In eval_list_method, the sum() path adds integers with int_sum += n without checked arithmetic. For large valid int lists this can overflow i64. In debug builds this panics; in release it can wrap to incorrect values, violating the interpreters overflow error semantics used by other integer arithmetic operations.

Potential impact: Calling .sum() on large integer lists can crash the interpreter or return silently incorrect totals.

Suggested fix: Replace unchecked integer addition with checked_add and return RuntimeError when overflow occurs, consistent with other numeric operations.
notes: Fix both locations: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/call/methods.rs and crates/ttrpg_interp/src/call/collection_builtins.rs
title: Bug: list.sum() uses unchecked i64 accumulation

Converting tdsl-naf6 cleanly therefore requires either adding a dedicated .ttrpg source file for the script to load, or extending the CLI runner with inline snippet support for script files.
title: CLI scripts cannot embed inline DSL source for return integration conversion

description: parse_pattern starts by matching on self.peek().clone(), then clones string and identifier payloads again in match arms. This increases allocation and copying in a hot parser path. close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/eval/control.rs

Why this is a bug: In eval_list_comprehension, each matched item pushes a scope, but two fallible calls return early before cleanup (eval_expr for filter and eval_expr for element). If either errors, the function exits without env.pop_scope, leaving an extra scope frame.

Potential impact: A runtime error inside a list comprehension can leave interpreter scope state inconsistent, so later evaluation may see incorrect bindings or leaked temporary values.

Suggested fix: Make scope cleanup unconditional for each iteration, for example by storing intermediate Results and popping before propagation, or by using an RAII scope guard that always pops on drop.
title: Bug: List comprehension leaks scope on eval errors

close_reason: Already fixed — floor/ceil properly reject NaN/inf/overflow with RuntimeError + regression tests
description: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/builtins.rs:43 and :57 cast f64 to i64 directly after floor()/ceil() using an as-cast. Rust float-to-int casts saturate and map NaN to 0, so out-of-range or non-finite inputs produce silent wrong integers instead of runtime errors (for example ceil(1e309) yields i64::MAX and floor(NaN) yields 0). This bypasses normal overflow/invalid-number protections and can corrupt downstream logic. floor/ceil should validate finite and in-range values before conversion and return RuntimeError when conversion is invalid.
title: floor/ceil silently saturate or coerce invalid floats to int

close_reason: Implemented and verified
description: MemoriseSpell and CastSpell trust the caller-supplied spell_level and never verify it against the actual level of the chosen SpellId. That allows implemented spells to be placed into or cast from the wrong slot level, which violates OSRIC's same-level memorization requirement. Reference: OSRIC PG §1.8.1 and class spellcasting rules in Chapter 3. Affected files: osric/osric_spells.ttrpg, osric/osric_cleric_spells.ttrpg, osric/osric_mu_spells.ttrpg, osric/osric_spell_registry.ttrpg.
title: Validate memorized slot level against the spell's true level

close_reason: Closed
description: When using `if let some(x) = opt_val` where the option's inner type is an enum, the checker interprets the binding variable `x` as an attempted enum variant match and rejects it with 'unknown identifier x in match on enum'. This is because the checker has a guard that prevents accidentally creating a variable when an enum variant is renamed — but it fires incorrectly in the if-let-some destructuring context. Workaround: use `opt.is_some()` + `opt.unwrap()` instead. Need to find a way to preserve the renamed-variant protection while allowing if-let bindings. Possibly: only apply the enum-variant check in match arms, not in if-let patterns; or use a different syntax to distinguish bindings from variant names.
title: if-let-some with enum inner type incorrectly rejects binding variable as enum variant

close_reason: Fixed all 5 interpreter/checker name resolution divergences
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/call/dispatch.rs

Why this is a bug: eval_call dispatches bare identifier calls in this order: variant then ordinal/from_ordinal/try_from_ordinal then collection builtins then condition then function. The checker (check_call) resolves in a different order: condition then ordinal/collection builtins then variant then function. Condition names are not rejected when they collide with builtins or variants, so a program that type-checks as a condition call can execute a builtin or variant path at runtime instead.

Potential impact: Valid, checked scripts can execute the wrong callable or fail at runtime with type or arity errors when a condition name collides with ordinal/from_ordinal/try_from_ordinal, collection builtins, or a variant name.

Suggested fix: Make runtime dispatch precedence match checker precedence for bare identifier calls (condition first, then special builtins, then variant, then function), or forbid these cross-namespace collisions during collection/checking.
title: Bug: eval_call dispatch order disagrees with checker for colliding names

close_reason: Already fixed in current codebase
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/lib.rs

Why this is a bug: Interpreter::new_with_invocation_start accepts any u64 start value, including u64::MAX. The next call to alloc_invocation_id performs checked_add(1).expect("invocation ID counter overflow"), which panics instead of returning a runtime error. This is a panic path reachable from valid API inputs.

Potential impact: A host restoring persisted state near or at the numeric limit can crash the process during normal action/reaction execution when an invocation ID is allocated.

Suggested fix: Handle overflow without panicking (for example, make allocation fallible and return RuntimeError, or reject start == u64::MAX in new_with_invocation_start with a clear error).
title: Bug: Invocation ID allocation panics at u64::MAX

close_reason: Not a bug: checker's dynamic while-loop and runtime's pre-computed bitmap are algorithmically equivalent. Both correctly skip named-arg slots. Tests confirm.
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_args.rs

Why this is a bug: check_args assigns positional arguments using only parameters already seen in prior arguments (tracked via satisfied). It does not reserve slots for named arguments that appear later in the call. For calls like f(1, a: 2), positional arg 1 is bound to a, then a: 2 is flagged as duplicate. The project semantics are fill-the-gaps based on the full set of named args, so 1 should bind to the leftmost unclaimed slot (b).

Potential impact: Valid calls mixing positional and named arguments can be rejected with false duplicate/type errors, and checker behavior diverges from runtime argument binding.

Suggested fix: Precompute all named-argument parameter indices before positional mapping (as done in runtime bind_args), then make positional arguments skip those reserved indices.
title: Bug: check_args mismaps mixed positional/named args

title: Monk thief skills table

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_pattern.rs

Why this is a bug: In check_pattern_inner, the PatternKind::Some(inner) branch calls self.check_pattern(...), which always resets in_destructure=false and is_binding_context=false. That drops caller context for nested patterns. As a result, inside destructuring or for-loop/comprehension binding patterns, bare identifiers inside some(...) are no longer treated as bindings as intended, and can be misclassified as enum variants or rejected as unknown enum identifiers.

Potential impact: Valid patterns are rejected and expected bindings are not introduced when identifier names overlap variant names. This causes false diagnostics and can break match/loop pattern-checking in real programs using option subpatterns.

Suggested fix: In the PatternKind::Some(inner) branch, recurse with check_pattern_inner(inner, ..., in_destructure, is_binding_context) for both normal and Ty::Error paths so context is preserved through nested option patterns.
title: Bug: some(...) pattern loses binding/destructure context

close_reason: Implemented and verified

Reference: OSRIC GM Guide §2.13.3.1. Affected file: osric/osric_scrolls.ttrpg.
title: Use class-specific minimum levels for spell-scroll failure chances

description: In crates/ttrpg_interp/src/call.rs lines 506-512, evaluate_fn_with_values pushes a scope before evaluating a missing parameter default, then calls eval_expr with ? before env.pop_scope. If default evaluation fails, the function returns early and the pushed scope is never popped. This leaves env.scopes unbalanced and can corrupt later evaluations in the same interpreter session. Repro: define a derive or mechanic default expression that errors, then call evaluate_derive or evaluate_mechanic without that argument.
title: evaluate_fn_with_values leaks scope on default eval errors

close_reason: Fixed all 5 interpreter/checker name resolution divergences
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/call/dispatch.rs

Why this is a bug: dispatch_alias_call resolves variant constructors with type_env.unique_variant_owner(name), which requires the variant name to be globally unique across all enums. The checker resolves alias-qualified calls against the alias target system and allows variants that are unique within that system even if the same variant exists in another system. This means alias calls accepted by the checker can still fail at runtime.

Potential impact: Valid alias-qualified constructor calls (for example Core.damage(...) where damage is unique in Core but also exists in another system) can raise runtime errors: no function, condition, or variant in module alias.

Suggested fix: In dispatch_alias_call, resolve the alias target system from the object identifier and select variant owners within that system (matching checker logic), or pass the resolved enum name from the field-access branch into alias dispatch.
title: Bug: Alias variant call uses global uniqueness instead of alias target

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_ast/src/diagnostic.rs

Why this is a bug: render_diagnostic computes caret_len as span.end - span.start and always prints the carets on a single source line. For spans that cross a newline, this includes bytes from subsequent lines and newline characters, so the underline is rendered on the first line with an incorrect (often very long) width.

Potential impact: Diagnostics for valid multiline spans point to the wrong region and can become misleading or noisy, reducing debuggability.

Suggested fix: When rendering single-line output, cap the underline length to the remaining bytes in the start line (line_text_end - local_start). Optionally add dedicated multiline rendering for cross-line spans.
title: Bug: Multiline spans render incorrect caret underline

close_reason: Duplicate of tdsl-ebm8. Same spawn duplicate-group issue at two layers; fix at parse layer makes mutation layer moot.
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_cli/src/runner/mutation.rs

Why this is a bug: In cmd_spawn, inline groups are collected into prepared_groups and then written in order without duplicate-name validation. If the same group appears twice in one spawn block, both entries pass validation and the later write silently overwrites the earlier one. The same parser path already rejects duplicate base fields, so this creates inconsistent and ambiguous input handling.

Potential impact: User mistakes in spawn blocks can go unnoticed, producing unexpected initial entity state and hard-to-debug setup behavior.

Suggested fix: Reject duplicate inline group names during spawn parsing/preparation (for example, track seen group names and return an error on repeat before state mutation).
title: Bug: Spawn accepts duplicate inline groups and silently overwrites

close_reason: Duration::Indefinite is sufficient for v0. Permanent traits are adequately modeled with entity fields + derives or indefinite conditions. No concrete use case requires machine-enforced irremovability yet — can revisit if pain point emerges during OSRIC modeling.
description: Weapon specialisation, infravision, and class abilities are permanent modifiers that shouldn't be removable via remove_condition(). Currently all conditions are equally removable. Workaround: use entity fields + derives, but this bypasses the modify pipeline. Alternative: add permanent flag to apply_condition() where permanent conditions reject remove_condition() calls. Or: new 'trait' keyword that behaves like a condition (has modify clauses) but is structurally irremovable — only revocable via revoke(invocation). Lower priority than other design items — entity fields + derives handle most OSRIC cases adequately.
title: Design: permanent/irremovable condition flag (traits)

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_literal.rs

Why this is a bug: check_struct_lit validates struct spread base expressions using strict equality (base_ty != result_ty) instead of compatibility. In action/reaction scope, turn is typed as builtin Ty::TurnBudget, while a user-defined struct TurnBudget literal has type Ty::Struct("TurnBudget"). These are intentionally treated as compatible elsewhere, so this path emits a false error for valid code.

Potential impact: Valid struct spread expressions like TurnBudget { ..turn } are rejected with a contradictory diagnostic (it can display both sides as TurnBudget). This blocks legitimate programs using custom TurnBudget schemas.

Suggested fix: Replace the strict equality check with types_compatible(&base_ty, &result_ty) (or equivalent compatibility logic) when validating struct literal base expressions.
title: Bug: Struct spread base check rejects equivalent TurnBudget types

close_reason: Closed
description: File crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_modify.rs. In check_modify_clause (lines 53-69), receiver with-groups are validated and narrowed via validate_with_groups. In check_suppress_clause (lines 271-356), receiver is bound (lines 282-289) but validate_with_groups is never called and receiver_with_groups is not passed in. Concrete impact: invalid receiver with-group names can pass with no diagnostic when a condition only has suppress clauses, and suppress binding expressions can incorrectly fail optional-group access checks because narrowing for declared with-groups is missing.

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/call/collection_builtins.rs

Why this is a bug: Most builtins in this file only check for too-few args (for example args.is_empty() or args.len() < 2) and then evaluate only the first required arguments, silently ignoring extras. This contradicts declared builtin arities and checker behavior (which expects exact counts), and it means calls like len(x, y) can succeed instead of erroring.

Potential impact: In unchecked expression-evaluation paths, invalid calls are accepted, extra argument expressions are skipped, and expected side effects or errors from those expressions do not occur.

Suggested fix: Enforce exact arity in each builtin (args.len() == required count) and return an arity RuntimeError when the count does not match.
title: Bug: Collection builtins accept and ignore extra arguments

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_parser/src/parser.rs

Why this is a bug: recover_to_decl() uses is_decl_start() to stop at the next declaration after an error, but is_decl_start() does not include the group keyword while parse_decl() does parse group declarations. During recovery, valid group declarations can be skipped instead of being parsed.

Potential impact: After a parse error inside a system block, subsequent valid group declarations may be dropped, causing cascading diagnostics and missing AST nodes for later compilation stages.

Suggested fix: Add "group" to is_decl_start() so recovery and declaration parsing recognize the same declaration starters.
title: Bug: Recovery skips group declarations

title: Migrate OSE code: add restricted to resource fields

description:
The interpreter currently uses a callback-based EffectHandler API because the evaluator is a synchronous recursive tree-walker over borrowed program state. We want to preserve the option to support a pull-based step/respond execution model in the future, especially for non-Rust hosts and other foreign embedding scenarios.

This issue tracks the architectural refactor needed to make that practical without destabilizing current integrations.

Goals:
- Introduce a single internal effect-emission seam instead of calling handler.handle() directly throughout the evaluator.
- Separate durable execution state from host interaction so execution can pause at effect boundaries and resume later.
- Design an owning Execution/Continuation object that can poll for the next effect and accept a response.
- Keep synchronous read APIs for host state; suspend only on effect boundaries.
- Preserve the current callback API as a compatibility shim layered on top of the new runner.

Likely implementation direction:
- Convert effectful execution paths to explicit frames/state machines rather than trying to suspend arbitrary recursive Rust call stacks.
- Start with action lifecycle, block execution, and emit/hook execution, which currently rely on stack-shaped cleanup and nested effect sequencing.
- Keep pure query paths like what_triggers() synchronous unless a concrete need emerges.

Risks / constraints:
- Current Env state includes scopes, invocation context, emit depth, lifecycle state, and return propagation, all of which must survive suspension safely.
- Semantics like guaranteed ActionCompleted emission and condition on_remove cleanup must remain correct across pause/resume boundaries.
- Thread-based approaches are explicitly not preferred; the target is an internal resumable runner that can also serve foreign-language hosts later.

Definition of done:
- There is a concrete internal execution abstraction that supports poll/respond stepping.
- The public callback API is implemented as a thin adapter over that abstraction.
- Existing behavior and protocol validation semantics remain unchanged.
title: Introduce resumable step-based execution API for the interpreter

description: Remaining OSRIC spells include Polymorph Self, Polymorph Other, Flesh to Stone/Stone to Flesh, Reincarnation, Shape Change, Statue, and related effects. Current entities have fixed schemas and the DSL/runtime lacks a reusable way to replace or remap an entity's form while preserving identity, stats that should carry over, and stats that should be recomputed. Add a transformation model suitable for spell-driven form changes.
notes:
## Potential Implementation Strategy: Suspend + Summon

Brainstormed approach: model complex transformations by composing two other planned features — suspended/off-board entities (tdsl-4qxs) and summon lifecycle (tdsl-ftim).

### Pattern
1. Suspend the original entity (off-board, preserved)
2. Spawn a form entity with the original's HP but the new form's stats
3. Link form back to original via a Polymorphed condition
4. On dispel/duration end: despawn form, restore original, sync HP

### Spell-to-model mapping
- **Polymorph Other**: Suspend + Summon (daily identity-loss check as condition hook)
- **Shape Change**: Suspend + Summon with free re-summoning of different forms
- **Polymorph Object**: Suspend + Summon with duration factor table
- **Polymorph Self**: Condition overlay only (just movement modes change, keep own stats/AC/attacks)
- **Flesh to Stone**: Suspend only (no form spawned); Stone to Flesh = restore + system shock
- **Statue**: Condition toggle (shift in/out freely, retain senses + HP)
- **Reincarnation**: Direct entity mutation (not a transformation — reroll physical stats for new race, keep mental stats + XP)

### Why this works
- Reuses tdsl-4qxs (suspend) and tdsl-ftim (summon lifecycle) — no new language concepts needed
- Identity is clean: original entity preserved perfectly, no field remapping
- Form entity is a real entity so `is` checks, condition stacking, and combat code all work naturally
- The link between original and form can be a Polymorphed condition with `original: entity` field
- Restore = on_remove hook of Polymorphed condition, syncs HP back

### Dependencies
Try this approach once tdsl-4qxs and tdsl-ftim (and their deps tdsl-i5bf, tdsl-420m) are closed.
title: Add transformation/form-change support for polymorph-class spells

close_reason: Implemented until_next_use and until_save duration patterns in expanded example. Added ConsumeOnUse (modify_applied) and UntilSaveCheck (turn_end + conditions()) hooks. Fixed checker to support Ty::Duration in pattern matching and variable bindings in enum destructuring sub-patterns.
description: The Duration enum covers time-based and indefinite durations but not event-triggered removal. The Help action needs 'remove this condition after it applies once' (next-use). Many spell effects need 'remove when the target succeeds on a saving throw at end of turn' (until-save). These require host-side duration interpretation beyond simple countdown.
notes:
## Analysis (2026-02-28)

### Current State
Duration is a ruleset-customizable enum — rulesets can already add variants like `until_next_use` and `until_save`. The interpreter is fully opaque to Duration semantics (stores value on ActiveCondition, passes through Effect::ApplyCondition). So the variants can exist today, but the host bears all burden of knowing when to remove them.

What's missing is any DSL-level mechanism for a condition to express 'I expire when X happens.'

### Could emit/events solve this?
The emit/hook system fires hooks inline and synchronously, and hooks can call remove_condition(). Conceptually:

```
condition Helped(source: Character) on bearer: Character {
    modify attack_roll + 1d4 {
        emit ConditionUsed(condition: self, bearer: bearer)
    }
}
on ConditionUsed(condition, bearer) {
    remove_condition(bearer, condition)
}
```

**Problems with this approach:**
1. No `self` reference in conditions — modify blocks can't reference their own identity for removal
2. Modify blocks can't emit — they're expression-level (pure), not statement-level
3. Ordering risk — removal during modifier evaluation could invalidate the modifier stack mid-resolution
4. `until_save` doesn't map cleanly — no modifier-fires event for saves; it's host-driven end-of-turn logic

### Possible Directions
- **Duration clause on conditions** referencing events: `duration: until ConditionUsed`
- **Host-interpreted variants** (status quo — add enum variants, host handles lifecycle)
- **Hybrid** where DSL expresses intent (`until_next_use`) and runtime has built-in support for consuming conditions after modifier application

Emit works conceptually for `until_next_use` with extensions but is awkward for `until_save`. No decision made yet.
title: Language: 'until next use' and 'until save' duration variants

description: Weapons and armor are always function parameters, never entity state. This blocks two-weapon fighting (main-hand vs off-hand), weapon proficiency enforcement at attack time, per-class weapon list validation (tdsl-q0pg), shield tracking, and weapon specialisation bonuses. Proposed: add wielded_main/wielded_off/worn_armor/worn_shield fields to Character entity. This changes the calling convention of all combat actions from 'pass weapon as arg' to 'read from entity'. Significant architectural decision — affects all existing combat code in osric_combat.ttrpg and osric_initiative.ttrpg. Should be decided before Advanced Combat epic work begins. Consider: option types for empty slots, equip/unequip actions, interaction with encumbrance.
design:
## Equipment State Design

### New Types (osric_equipment.ttrpg)

```
enum WieldedItem {
    Melee(weapon: MeleeWeapon)
    Missile(weapon: MissileWeapon)
    // Future: Wand, Rod, Staff-as-implement, etc.
}

struct Armor {
    armour_type: ArmourType
    // Future: magical_bonus, instance_id, name, etc.
}

struct Shield {
    shield_type: ShieldType
    // Future: magical_bonus, instance_id, etc.
}
```

### Character Entity Changes (osric_core.ttrpg)

Add fields:
    wielded_main: option<WieldedItem> = none
    wielded_off: option<WieldedItem> = none
    worn_armor: option<Armor> = none
    worn_shield: option<Shield> = none

Remove fields (replaced by derives):
    armor_ac: int = 10
    shield_ac_bonus: int = 0
    armour_movement_cap: Feet = 0ft

### New Derives

Equipment extraction helpers (osric_equipment.ttrpg):
    wielded_melee(char) -> option<MeleeWeapon>     — extract Melee variant from wielded_main
    wielded_missile(char) -> option<MissileWeapon>  — extract Missile variant from wielded_main
    wielded_off_melee(char) -> option<MeleeWeapon>  — extract Melee variant from wielded_off

AC/movement derives (osric_combat.ttrpg, near effective_target_ac):
    base_armor_ac(char) -> int          — worn_armor → armour_def().ascending_ac, else 10
    base_shield_bonus(char) -> int      — worn_shield → shield_def().ac_bonus, else 0
    armor_movement_cap(char) -> Feet    — worn_armor → armour_def().movement_cap, else 0ft

effective_target_ac updated to call base_armor_ac / base_shield_bonus instead of reading entity fields.

### Equip/Unequip Functions (osric_equipment.ttrpg)

Hands validation rules:
    - Two-handed main weapon → off-hand must be none, shield must be none
    - Shield → off-hand must be none
    - Off-hand weapon → shield must be none, main must not be two-handed

function equip_main(char, item: WieldedItem)
    If item is two-handed (check via weapon def): clear off-hand, clear shield.
    Set wielded_main. Update current_weight.

function equip_off(char, item: WieldedItem)
    Validate main isn't two-handed. Clear shield.
    Set wielded_off. Update current_weight.

function equip_armor(char, armor: Armor)
    Set worn_armor. Update current_weight + encumbrance.

function equip_shield(char, shield: Shield)
    Validate off-hand is free. Set worn_shield. Update current_weight.

function unequip_main/off/armor/shield(char)
    Clear slot. Update current_weight (+ encumbrance for armor).

### Combat Action Changes (osric_combat.ttrpg)

Actions lose weapon parameter, read from entity state:
    MeleeAttack(target, weapon)  → MeleeAttack(target)    — reads wielded_melee(attacker)
    MissileAttack(target, weapon, distance) → MissileAttack(target, distance) — reads wielded_missile(attacker)
    Charge(target, weapon)       → Charge(target)          — reads wielded_melee(attacker)

requires blocks validate weapon is equipped.
Mechanics (resolve_melee_attack etc.) keep weapon param — pure computation, no entity read.

### Initiative System Changes (osric_initiative.ttrpg)

RoundAction keeps weapon_melee/weapon_missile fields (needed for speed factor calc at declaration time).

resolve_action flow changes:
    1. Equip declared weapon (equip_main) before calling action
    2. Action reads weapon from entity state
    3. No weapon param in action call

### Migration Notes

- All code setting armor_ac / shield_ac_bonus / armour_movement_cap directly must migrate to equip_armor / equip_shield calls
- REPL spawn commands that set these fields need updating
- Test files that set raw AC values need updating
- Encumbrance derive (character_encumbrance) needs update if it reads armour_movement_cap field
title: Design: equipment/wielded state on Character entity

close_reason: Already fixed in current codebase
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/reference_state.rs

Why this is a bug: GameState::add_condition preserves non-zero cond.id values but does not advance next_condition_id to stay above preserved IDs. After loading/inserting existing conditions with explicit IDs, later auto-assigned conditions (via apply_condition or add_condition with id=0) can reuse IDs that already exist.

Potential impact: Duplicate condition IDs break uniqueness assumptions used for deduplication and identity-based removal, leading to skipped/merged condition processing or removing the wrong condition instance.

Suggested fix: When inserting a condition with non-zero id, update next_condition_id = max(next_condition_id, cond.id + 1) using saturating arithmetic.
title: Bug: condition id counter not advanced for preassigned IDs

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/action.rs

Why this is a bug: In collect_and_apply_cost_modifiers(), binding matching sets param_val to Value::Entity(*actor) for every binding, regardless of binding.name. For modify Action.cost clauses that bind an action parameter (not the receiver), runtime compares the evaluated binding expression against the actor entity instead of the actual argument value, so explicit parameter bindings cannot match correctly.

Potential impact: Condition-based cost modifiers with parameter constraints are skipped or evaluated incorrectly, causing wrong cost deduction behavior in valid programs.

Suggested fix: Resolve each binding.name to the target call value (receiver -> actor, param names -> currently bound argument values) before comparison, mirroring the generic modify binding logic used in pipeline.rs.
title: Bug: Cost modify bindings compare against actor for all names

close_reason: Closed
description: In crates/ttrpg_cli/src/format.rs line 11, Value::Str is formatted with a raw interpolation inside quotes: format!("\"{}\"", s). Embedded quotes, backslashes, and control characters are not escaped. Example: Value::Str("a\"b") renders as "a"b" (ambiguous/unbalanced), and Value::Str("x\ny") emits a literal newline into output. This also affects path rendering at lines 105-108 because format_path delegates index formatting to format_value, so indexed string keys can produce malformed paths/log lines. Suggested fix: use escaped string-literal formatting (for example debug-style escaping) for Value::Str.
title: format_value emits unescaped strings in CLI output

close_reason: Closed
description: crates/ttrpg_parser/src/lower.rs builds existing_names from Derive/Mechanic/Action/Reaction/Prompt only (lines 35-44). Hook and Table declarations are omitted even though they are collected in the callable function namespace during checking, so synthetic mechanic names are not collision-checked against those declarations. This produces inconsistent diagnostics and allows lowering to proceed into duplicate-function errors later instead of reporting the intended synthetic-name collision at lowering time. Include Hook and Table names in the pre-lowering collision set.
title: Synthetic mechanic collision check misses table/hook declarations

close_reason: Fixed in commit 313628b (Fix 4 REPL/CLI bugs)
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_cli/src/runner/load.rs

Why this is a bug: cmd_load returns early on glob expansion errors (invalid pattern or no matches) before calling load_paths. Those early returns skip the stale-state reset performed in load_paths error paths. As a result, a failed load due to glob resolution leaves the previous program, handles, and last_paths intact.

Potential impact: After a failed load with a bad glob, commands can continue operating on the old program, and reload loads old files instead of the failed target set. This is inconsistent with other load failure modes and can cause users to execute against unintended game rules.

Suggested fix: Route glob-resolution failures through the same failure-state handling as other load errors, or perform equivalent state reset plus last_paths update in cmd_load before returning.
title: Bug: load glob errors keep stale program and reload target

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_parser/src/stmt.rs

Why this is a bug: parse_stmt() unconditionally routes leading identifiers named grant/emit to dedicated statement parsers, and routes revoke unless followed by '('. These are soft keywords (still tokenized as Ident), so valid statements starting with identifiers named grant/emit/revoke (for example assignments or non-call expression statements) are parsed as grant/revoke/emit statements and fail. This contradicts soft-keyword behavior already validated elsewhere (e.g. hook usable as identifier).

Potential impact: Valid programs are rejected with parse diagnostics when users have variables/fields/callees named grant/emit/revoke and use them at statement start, causing incorrect parse failures and skipped statements.

Suggested fix: In parse_stmt(), only dispatch to parse_grant_stmt/parse_revoke_stmt/parse_emit_stmt when lookahead matches each statement form (e.g. grant/revoke followed by an expression that can form entity.Group with required trailing syntax; emit followed by Ident then '('). Otherwise fall back to generic expression/assignment parsing.
title: Bug: Statement soft-keyword dispatch rejects valid identifier statements

close_reason: Implemented and verified
description: OSRIC PG ancestry multiclass rules say elves and half-elves use the least restrictive armour restrictions across their classes (for example, fighter/magic-user may wear plate; magic-user/thief may wear studded leather). In osric/osric_multiclass.ttrpg, effective_armour_permission() ranks permissions from NoArmour to AnyArmour and then takes the minimum rank, which enforces the most restrictive rule instead. This blocks legal multiclass equipment usage and contradicts the quoted examples. Reference: PG §1.3.11 and ancestry multiclass notes in §1.2.2.3 and §1.2.4.3. Affected file: osric/osric_multiclass.ttrpg.
title: Use least-restrictive multiclass armour permissions

close_reason: Fixed: bare idents in pattern position now fall through to variable binding when not a known variant, regardless of enum scrutinee type
description: The spec says bare IDENT in pattern position should be a variable binding if it doesn't match an enum variant in scope. Currently, the checker rejects non-variant identifiers with 'unknown identifier X in match on enum Y; if this is meant as a catch-all, use _'. This affects both match arms (e.g. 'other => 0') and for-loop patterns (e.g. 'for c in colors'). The for-loop case is especially impactful since there's no workaround — you can't iterate over an enum-typed list and bind each element to a variable. Root cause is likely in the checker's pattern resolution logic which treats all bare idents as attempted variant matches when the scrutinee/element type is an enum, rather than falling back to binding.
title: Pattern binding fails for enum-typed scrutinees in match/for

close_reason: All implementation complete: AST/parser, checker, interpreter, budget enforcement, integration tests. Only remaining sub-issue is tdsl-xzqg (docs, P3).
description:
Functions cannot call costed actions (e.g. MeleeAttack with cost { attack }) because they lack access to the turn budget. This forces functions like resolve_action in osric_initiative.ttrpg to duplicate action logic by calling underlying mechanics directly instead of reusing existing actions.

Need a mechanism for functions to set up a turn budget before calling an action on behalf of a combatant. Possible approaches:
- A builtin like with_budget(entity, TurnBudget { attack: 1 }) { entity.MeleeAttack(...) }
- Implicit budget provisioning when a function calls a costed action
- A block-scoped turn budget declaration

This would let resolve_round call MeleeAttack/MissileAttack/Charge directly instead of reimplementing their logic.
notes: Design doc: spec/design/with_budget-quibbled.md (supersedes with_budget.md). Key change from original: OSRIC examples corrected — combatant is the receiver (actor), not the target. cost_payer mechanism retained for summoning/delegation patterns where receiver ≠ payer. close_reason: FALSE POSITIVE: agent verified at_ident() check + fallthrough works fine — grant/revoke as variable names are not blocked
description:
In crates/ttrpg_parser/src/stmt.rs, parse_stmt() unconditionally dispatches to parse_grant_stmt() / parse_revoke_stmt() whenever the first token is Ident("grant") or Ident("revoke") (lines 44-50).

grant and revoke are soft keywords (lexer emits them as TokenKind::Ident; see crates/ttrpg_lexer/src/token.rs line 23 and crates/ttrpg_lexer/src/lexer.rs lines 152-163). This incorrectly rejects valid identifier-led statements like: grant = 1, revoke += 1, grant(actor).

Actual behavior: parser enters parse_grant_stmt/parse_revoke_stmt, then errors (for example expecting entity.GroupName), and recovery drops the statement.
Expected behavior: only parse grant/revoke statements when following tokens match dedicated grant/revoke statement forms; otherwise fall back to normal expression/assignment statement parsing.
title: Parser treats grant/revoke identifiers as mandatory statements at stmt start

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_cli/src/runner/util.rs

Why this is a bug: split_first_token finds the first whitespace byte index with s.find(char::is_whitespace), then slices the remainder as &s[pos + 1..]. For multi-byte Unicode whitespace (for example U+00A0), pos + 1 is not a UTF-8 character boundary, which causes a runtime panic when slicing.

Potential impact: Valid user input containing non-ASCII whitespace can crash CLI command handling instead of returning a parse/usage error.

Suggested fix: Advance by the matched whitespace character length (len_utf8) rather than a hard-coded +1, mirroring the safer implementation used in crates/ttrpg_cli/src/commands.rs.
title: Bug: split_first_token panics on Unicode whitespace

close_reason: Fixed with regression tests
description:
In crates/ttrpg_lexer/src/lexer.rs, oversized numeric components are silently converted to wrong values: parse failures use unwrap_or(0) and dice count is narrowed with `as u32`.

Concrete paths:
- line 65: INT parse overflow -> Int(0) (and UnitLiteral value 0 at line 101)
- line 77: dice sides parse overflow -> sides = 0
- line 134: dice filter amount parse overflow -> n = 0
- line 85: dice count cast (i64 -> u32) wraps modulo 2^32 (example: 4294967296d6 becomes count 0)

This masks malformed/overflowing input as valid tokens and changes program behavior without diagnostics.

File/lines: crates/ttrpg_lexer/src/lexer.rs:65,77,85,101,134.
title: Numeric literal overflow in lexer is silently coerced/wrapped instead of erroring

close_reason: Not a bug: code explicitly sets bearer=entity before storing. No path to mismatch.
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/reference_state.rs

Why this is a bug: WritableState::add_condition takes both a target entity and an ActiveCondition, but GameState::add_condition stores cond as-is without enforcing cond.bearer == *entity. This allows conditions to be attached under one entity key while carrying a different bearer value, which violates internal state consistency. Runtime condition evaluation binds receiver/suppress logic from condition.bearer, so mismatched data can make logic run against the wrong entity.

Potential impact: Conditions may modify/suppress behavior for the wrong entity, causing incorrect rule outcomes that are hard to debug because storage location and bearer disagree.

Suggested fix: In add_condition, set cond.bearer = *entity before storing (or reject mismatches explicitly).
title: Bug: add_condition can store mismatched bearer for target entity

close_reason: Full stacking/exclusivity control implemented: AST, parser, checker, interpreter (all 3 collection paths), integration tests, OSRIC conditions annotated
description: Currently multiple conditions modifying the same derive use strict last-writer-wins (oldest condition first). This works for additive modifiers (Prone +4 + RearAttacked +2 stack because both increment attack_mod). But fails for: concealment not stacking with invisibility (PG p96), exclusive damage resistances, qualitative overrides (AutoHit vs attack_bonus). Possible designs: (A) stack_group attribute on modify clauses for exclusivity, (B) priority levels, (C) additive vs override semantics per field. Lower priority for OSRIC since most modifiers are additive and the few non-stacking cases can be handled with guard conditions in the modify clauses themselves.
design:
## `stacking` Clause — Implementation-Ready Design

### Syntax

```
condition <Name>(<params>) on <receiver>: <type>
    stacking <policy>
{
    ...
}
```

### Policies

| Policy | Meaning | Use case |
|--------|---------|----------|
| `all` | Every instance contributes all effects (default, implicit) | Grappling with different opponents |
| `first` | Oldest instance wins; others suppressed | Prone, Stunned — parameterless non-doubling |
| `best by highest(<param>) ties oldest` | Highest param value wins; tie → oldest | Concealed(level), Cover(penalty) |
| `best by lowest(<param>) ties oldest` | Lowest param value wins; tie → oldest | Future: conditions where smaller = stronger |

`ties oldest` is the only tie-breaker for now. Could add `ties newest` later.

### Semantics

For a given bearer, when multiple instances of the same condition exist:

1. Group all active instances by `(condition_name, bearer)`
2. Pick the winner per group according to the stacking policy
3. Suppress all non-winner instances entirely — skip their ancestors and all clause types (modify, suppress, suppress_modify, cost modifiers)

Suppressed instances remain in state. Duration keeps ticking. When the winner expires, next-best becomes the new winner on next evaluation.

### Semantic Details

- **Tie-breaking**: `gained_at` (oldest first). If `gained_at` ties, break by lowest `condition_id` for full determinism.
- **BestBy.param**: Must name an `int` parameter declared on that condition. Inherited parent params do not qualify unless the child also declares them.
- **Inheritance**: The `stacking` clause belongs to the concrete declared condition and is NOT merged across `extends`. Precedence is decided by the child condition's own policy. The winning instance still contributes inherited parent clauses.

### Shared Helper

A single helper computes the allowlist for all conditions on a bearer:

```rust
fn compute_stacking_winners(
    conditions: &[ActiveCondition],
    program: &Program,
) -> HashSet<u64>  // set of winning condition_ids
```

- For `All` policy: all instance ids are included (cheap)
- For `First`: oldest instance wins per (name, bearer) group
- For `BestBy`: highest/lowest param value wins, ties by gained_at then condition_id

The allowlist is computed once per bearer, then passed to all three collection paths.

### Winner Filter Placement

Apply the winner filter BEFORE clause collection, not after. If a condition instance loses precedence, skip its ancestor chain walk and all clause types entirely. Simpler and safer.

### Affected Code Paths

| Location | What it collects | Change |
|----------|-----------------|--------|
| `pipeline.rs` `collect_modifiers_owned` | modify + suppress_modify clauses | Check allowlist before walking ancestors |
| `action.rs` | cost modifiers | Check allowlist before collecting |
| `event.rs` | suppress clauses | Check allowlist before collecting |

### AST Representation

```rust
pub enum StackingPolicy {
    All,
    First,
    BestBy {
        param: Name,
        direction: Direction,
    },
}

pub enum Direction {
    Highest,
    Lowest,
}
```

Tie-breaking is always oldest (lowest gained_at, then lowest condition_id). Extend with TieBreaker enum later if needed.

### Not In Scope

- Cross-condition groups (Concealed vs Invisible) — separate feature
- `best by <expr>` — future extension, single param only for now
- Duplicate-scope (same source vs different source) — separate concept
- Lifecycle uniqueness (reject/replace on apply) — orthogonal to effect precedence
title: Design: modify clause stacking/exclusivity control

close_reason: revoke() was already allowed in function blocks via allows_mutation(), but error messages were misleading. Fixed error messages for revoke() and apply_condition/remove_condition to include 'function' in the allowed block list. Added positive checker test.
description:
Currently invocation() and revoke() permissions are tracked together via a single bool in the checker. Functions are blocked from both because they lack an actor for invocation(). But revoke(inv) doesn't need the current invocation context — it just takes an Invocation value and emits a RevokeInvocation effect. It should be allowed in functions so they can clean up conditions from stored invocation handles (e.g., ending concentration spells).

Implementation: split the single permission bool into separate flags for invocation() and revoke(), and allow revoke in function scope.
title: Allow revoke() in function blocks

close_reason: Segment-based initiative system complete: surprise, initiative rolls, segment assignment, fighter multi-attacks, spell timing, speed factor tie-breaking, movement, CastingSpell condition, SpellInterruption hook, charge interaction. Documented 4 DSL limitations.
description: This is the most mechanically distinctive aspect of OSRIC combat and a key stress test for the DSL. OSRIC uses a 10-segment combat round with d6 group initiative (low roll = earlier segment). Key mechanics to model: (1) Surprise: d6 per side, result 1-2 = surprised for that many segments. DEX surprise modifier adjusts segments. (2) Initiative: d6 per side, determines starting segment for actions. Low is better. (3) Segment resolution: actions resolve segment-by-segment. Movement happens at 1/10 rate per segment. Spells have casting times in segments (begin at initiative segment, complete after casting time). (4) Spell interruption: if caster takes damage before spell completes, spell is lost. (5) Missile weapons with DEX bonus may fire in earlier segments. (6) Fighter multiple attacks happen at segments 1 and 10. (7) Weapon speed factor breaks ties when initiative is simultaneous. This system may require: tracking current segment, ordered action queues, interruptible casting. Investigate what DSL features are needed vs what exists — this is exploratory. Document any missing language features discovered.
title: OSRIC: Segment-based initiative system

close_reason: Implemented and verified

Reference: OSRIC PG §1.3.4.3, §1.3.8.3, §1.3.9.3, and §1.6.1.3. Affected files: osric/osric_initiative.ttrpg, osric/osric_combat.ttrpg.
title: Fix fighter-group extra-attack level thresholds and timing

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_method.rs

Why this is a bug: types_compatible is defined as types_compatible(actual, expected) and includes one-way compatibility (ActiveCondition is accepted where Condition is expected). In this file, several method checks pass arguments in reverse order (expected first, actual second), including list .append() and set .add()/.remove()/.contains(). This causes false type errors for valid inputs such as passing ActiveCondition where Condition is expected.

Potential impact: Valid programs are incorrectly rejected by the checker when using list/set methods with one-way-compatible element types. This can block compilation for legitimate code paths.

Suggested fix: Swap types_compatible argument order to (actual, expected) in affected checks, e.g. use types_compatible(&elem_ty, &inner) for list append and types_compatible(&elem_ty, inner) for set element methods.
title: Bug: Reversed compatibility check in list/set methods rejects valid subtype args

close_reason: Fixed all 4 alias/shadow resolution bugs
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_parser/src/resolve.rs

Why this is a bug: all_import_targets is precomputed before per-import validation and includes every existing, non-self target. Later alias shadow checks use this set even if some imports are rejected (for example due to alias conflicts). That means a rejected import can still contribute exported names to shadow detection for other aliases, producing errors based on imports that are not actually accepted into sys_info.imports.

Potential impact: Spurious alias-shadow diagnostics can appear in otherwise valid imports when another import in the same system is invalid, creating misleading cascaded errors and obscuring the real root cause.

Suggested fix: Build shadow-check candidates from successfully accepted imports (or from imports that pass all non-shadow validation checks), rather than from the precomputed raw target set.
title: Bug: Alias shadow check includes rejected imports

description: The spec's cost token table lists only: action, bonus_action, reaction (singular forms mapping to TurnBudget fields). The implementation accepts these PLUS: actions, bonus_actions, reactions (plural forms), movement, and free_interactions. The error message confirms: 'expected one of: action, actions, bonus_action, bonus_actions, free_interactions, movement, reaction, reactions'. Decision needed: update the spec to document the full accepted vocabulary, or restrict the implementation. Recommend updating the spec since the broader set is useful (movement costs for flying, free_interactions for object interaction economy).
title: Cost token vocabulary is wider than spec documents

This makes control flow hard to reason about and increases regression risk when adding types. close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_modify.rs

Why this is a bug: check_modify_selector decides whether to compute a shared return type using stmt_uses_result, but that helper only flags result when it is an assignment target (result = ...) or a field assignment (result.<field> = ...). It does not inspect expression trees in let/if/param override values, so bodies that read result (for example param = result) are treated as not using it. The synthetic function then gets Ty::Unit as return type, causing incorrect type checking and false errors for valid selector modifies.

Potential impact: Valid selector-based modify clauses that read result can be rejected, and diagnostics point to wrong type expectations (Unit) instead of the actual shared return type.

Suggested fix: Replace stmt_uses_result with a recursive check that walks expression nodes inside all modify statements (condition/value expressions and nested blocks) and detects identifier reads of result, then use that to drive common return type validation.
title: Bug: Selector modify misses result reads in expressions

close_reason: Triaged: no measurable performance impact on realistic inputs. Small data sizes (3 outcomes, 5 options, 30 decls, 50 tokens) make algorithmic complexity irrelevant.
description: The completer allocates and clones repeatedly on each tab-completion call (e.g., merging derive/mechanic candidates into a new Vec<String>, cloning tuple key parts to query group_fields, and cloning full candidate lists in prefix_matches_owned when prefix is empty). This is in an interactive hot path and increases latency/GC pressure as completion context grows. Refactor to use borrowed lookups and iterator-based filtering where possible, and avoid rebuilding candidate vectors per call. Add/adjust tests or benchmarks to confirm behavior is unchanged.
title: crates/ttrpg_cli/src/completer.rs: Reduce per-keystroke allocations in completer hot path

description:
option<T> values cannot be destructured or unwrapped:
- No `some(x)` pattern in match: `match val { some(x) => x.field, none => default }`
- No `if let` syntax: `if let some(armor) = char.equipped_armor { ... }`
- No `.unwrap()` or `.unwrap_or(default)` methods
- Can't use option<Ability> as a map index without unwrapping first
- `none` is not a valid pattern (separate parser bug ttrpg_dsl-efg)

This blocks any idiomatic use of option fields on entities (equipped_armor, spellcasting_ability, etc.).
title: Language: option<T> destructuring and unwrapping

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_parser/src/lib.rs

Why this is a bug: parse_multi assigns file ids with FileId(i as u32). For i == u32::MAX, this produces the reserved sentinel FileId::SYNTH, and for larger i values it silently wraps/truncates. This creates non-unique or synthetic file ids for real files.

Potential impact: Diagnostics and span ownership can be attributed to the wrong file or treated as synthetic/no-source locations when loading very large source sets. This breaks correctness and can hide source locations.

Suggested fix: Use u32::try_from(i) and emit or propagate a hard diagnostic when conversion fails, and explicitly reject u32::MAX so real files never receive FileId::SYNTH.
title: Bug: parse_multi FileId truncation and SYNTH collision
